blocks:
Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
By the Manifest Book: (2)
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٣)
We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason, (3)
وَإِنَّهُۥ فِىٓ أُمِّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِىٌّ حَكِيمٌ (٤)
and it is sublime and wise with Us in the Mother Book. (4)
أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنكُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَ صَفْحًا أَن كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّسْرِفِينَ (٥)
Shall We keep back the Reminder from you and disregard you because you are an unrestrained lot? (5)
وَكَمْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن نَّبِىٍّ فِى ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٦)
How many a prophet We have sent to the former peoples! (6)
وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن نَّبِىٍّ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٧)
There did not come to them any prophet but that they used to deride him. (7)
فَأَهْلَكْنَآ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا وَمَضَىٰ مَثَلُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٨)
So We destroyed those who were stronger than these, and the example of the former peoples has already come to pass. (8)
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ خَلَقَهُنَّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٩)
If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘The All-mighty, the All-knowing created them.’ (9)
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٠)
He, who made the earth a cradle for you, and made for you in it ways so that you may be guided [to your destinations], (10)
وَٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءًۢ بِقَدَرٍ فَأَنشَرْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ (١١)
and who sent down water from the sky in a measured manner, and We revived with it a dead country. Likewise, you shall be raised [from the dead]. (11)
وَٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْفُلْكِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ مَا تَرْكَبُونَ (١٢)
He created all the kinds and made for you the ships and the cattle such which you ride, (12)
لِتَسْتَوُۥا۟ عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِۦ ثُمَّ تَذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ إِذَا ٱسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَتَقُولُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُۥ مُقْرِنِينَ (١٣)
that you may sit on their backs, then remember the blessing of your Lord when you are settled on them, and say, ‘Immaculate is He who has disposed this for us, and we [by ourselves] were no match for it. (13)
وَإِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا لَمُنقَلِبُونَ (١٤)
Indeed we shall return to our Lord.’ (14)

(Commentary)

The Surah is established for the purpose of warning (الإنذار), as evidenced by its opening and its conclusion, as well as the objectives interspersed between them—except for His saying: Except the God-conscious. O My servants! Today you shall have no fear [43:67-68] until the completion of six verses, which are parenthetical (استطرادية).

It mentions that the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established way of Allah in dealing with His creation — is the sending down of the Reminder (الذكر) and the dispatching of prophets and messengers. The people’s excess (إسراف) in their speech and actions does not deter Him from this; rather, He sends the prophets and messengers, destroys those who mock and deny them, and then drives them toward an eternal Fire.

It mentions the sending of the prophets in general first, then names among them Abraham, then Moses, then Jesus (peace be upon him). It mentions various forms of the disbelievers' excess, the foremost being their claim that Allah, the Glorified, has a son and that the angels are the daughters of Allah. Thus, there is a special emphasis on negating offspring from Him, the Exalted; He repeats this, refutes it, and threatens them with punishment. The Surah also contains other scattered truths.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the contents of its verses, except for His saying: Ask those of Our apostles whom We sent before you [43:45]; however, its exceptional status has not been established, as will be discussed, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: By the Manifest Book (وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ) [43:2]. Its apparent meaning is that it is an oath, and its response is His saying: Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran [43:3] until the end of the two verses. The Quran being "manifest" (مبين) refers to its making clear and manifest the path of guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — as the Exalted said: and We have sent down the Book to you as a clarification of all things (النحل: ٨٩). Or it refers to it being manifest in itself, such that there is no doubt concerning it, as He said: This is the Book, there is no doubt in it (البقرة: ٢).

His saying, the Exalted: Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason (إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ) [43:3]. The pronoun refers to the Book (الكتاب). "An Arabic Quran" (قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا) means recited in the Arabic language, and "so that you may exercise your reason" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ) is the ultimate end and purpose of this making (الجعل).

Making the hope of its being reasoned (تعقله) the end of the aforementioned making testifies that it possesses a stage of being and existence (الكينونة والوجود) that the human intellect (عقول الناس) cannot reach. It is the nature of the intellect (العقل) to grasp every conceptual matter (أمر فكري), no matter how subtle or precise it may be. Thus, the import of the verse is that the Book, according to its own station (موطنه) which it has in itself, is a matter beyond thought (وراء الفكر) and alien to human intellects. Allah only made it an Arabic Quran and clothed it in this garment in the hope that human intellects might become familiar with it and thus reason it. The "hope" (الرجاء) in the speech of the Exalted is grounded in the context or the addressee, rather than the Speaker, as has been mentioned more than once.

His saying, the Exalted: And indeed it is in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely sublime and wise (وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ) [43:4]. This is a confirmation and clarification of what the previous verse indicated: that the Book, in its original station, is beyond the grasp of intellects.

The pronoun refers to the Book. What is meant by the "Mother of the Book" (أُمِّ الْكِتابِ) is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), as the Exalted said: Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet (البروج: ٢٢). It is called the "Mother of the Book" because it is the source (أصل) of the heavenly books from which others are transcribed. The qualification "in the Mother of the Book" and "with Us" (لَدَيْنا) is for clarification, not for exclusion (الاحتراز). The meaning is: while it is in the Mother of the Book with Us—as an inseparable state—it is sublime (علي) and wise (حكيم). A discussion regarding the Mother of the Book will come at the end of Surah al-Jathiyah, Allah willing.

What is meant by it being "sublime" (علي), according to the import of the previous verse, is that it is of such high status and rank that intellects cannot reach it. By it being "wise" (حكيم), it means that there it is consolidated (محكم), not detailed (مفصل) nor divided into surahs, verses, sentences, and words, as it became after being made an Arabic Quran. This is derived from His saying, the Exalted: A Book whose verses have been consolidated and then elaborated from One who is All-wise, All-aware (هود: ١).

These two attributes—namely, its being sublime and wise—are what necessitate its being beyond human intellects. For the intellect, in its thinking, only grasps that which is of the nature of concepts (المفاهيم) and words (الألفاظ) first, and which is composed of propositional premises (مقدمات تصديقية) that follow one another, as is the case in the Quranic verses and sentences. However, if the matter is beyond concepts and words and is not divided into parts and sections, the intellect has no way to grasp it.

The summary of the meaning of the two verses is: The Book, with Us in the Preserved Tablet, possesses a sublime station and consolidation (أحكام) such that intellects cannot reach it due to those two attributes; We only sent it down by making it a recited Arabic [Book] in the hope that people might reason it.

If you say: The apparent meaning of His saying: so that you may exercise your reason (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ) is the possibility of people attaining a complete understanding (تعقلا تاما) of this descended Arabic Quran. Therefore, this which we recite and reason must either be completely identical (مطابقا) to what is in the Mother of the Book or not. The second option is certainly false; for how could it be so when the Exalted says: And indeed it is in the Mother of the Book and Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet (البروج: ٢٢) and Indeed it is a noble Quran, in a protected Book (الواقعة: ٧٨)? Thus, the first option is determined. But if it is completely identical to the Mother of the Book, what is the meaning of the Arabic Quran we have being reasoned by us, while that which is in the Mother of the Book with Allah is not reasoned by us?

I say: It is possible that the relationship between what we have and what is in the Mother of the Book is the relationship between the proverb (المثل) and that which it represents (الممثل). The proverb is the very thing represented, yet the one for whom the proverb is struck only understands the proverb. Understand this.

From what has passed, the weakness of the views mentioned by some in interpreting these two attributes becomes clear. For instance, some said: What is meant by it being "sublime" (علي) is that it is high in its eloquence and clarifies what people need. Others said: It means it surpasses every book by the miracle (الإعجاز) specific to it, and it abrogates other books while no book abrogates it. Others said: It means the angels and the believers venerate it.

Similarly, regarding the meaning of "wise" (حكيم), some said: It is a manifestation of profound wisdom (الحكمة البالغة). Others said: It means it speaks only with wisdom and says only truth and correctness; thus, describing it as "wise" is metaphorical (تجوز) for the purpose of hyperbole (المبالغة). The weakness of these views is evident upon reflecting on the import of the previous verse and the fact that its being made an Arabic Quran occurred through its descent from the Mother of the Book.

His saying, the Exalted: Shall We then turn away the Reminder from you, disregarding [you] because you are a transgressing people? (أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنْكُمُ الذِّكْرَ صَفْحاً أَنْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً مُسْرِفِينَ) [43:5]. The interrogative is for denial (الإنكار), and the "fa" (ف) is for branching (التفريع) from what preceded. "Striking the Reminder away from them" (ضرب الذكر عنهم) means turning it away from them. It is said in al-Majma': "The origin of 'I struck the reminder away from him' is when a rider is on a beast and wishes to turn it away from a direction, he strikes it with a stick or a whip to divert it to another direction; then 'striking' was used in place of 'turning away' and 'diverting'." End quote.

Disregarding (الصفح) means turning away (الإعراض), so "disregarding" (صفحا) is an object of purpose (مفعول له). It is also possible that it means "aside" (الجانب). The phrase "because you are" (أَنْ كُنْتُمْ) has a hidden preposition, the estimation being "because you were," and it is connected to His saying: "Shall We then strike away."

The meaning is: Shall We then turn away the Reminder from you—which is the Book We made a Quran so that you may reason it—by turning away from you because you are an extravagant people (مسرفين)? Or shall We turn it away from you to one side because you are an extravagant people? That is, We shall not turn it away from you for that reason.

His saying, the Exalted: How many a prophet have We sent among the ancients! And no prophet came to them but they used to mock him (وَكَمْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ نَبِيٍّ فِي الْأَوَّلِينَ وَما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ) [43:6-7]. "How many" (كَمْ) is for multiplicity (التكثير). The "ancients" (الأولون) are the passed nations. "And no prophet came to them..." is a circumstantial clause (حال), and its operator is "We sent."

These two verses and what follows them serve as a justification (تعليل) for not turning the Reminder away from them, by explaining that your being an extravagant people does not prevent Us from carrying out the sunnah of guidance through revelation (الوحي). For We have sent many a prophet among the past nations, and the situation was that no prophet came to them but they mocked him, and the matter ended with Us destroying those among them who were more powerful (أشد بطشا) than you.

Just as the end of their excess and mockery was destruction rather than the turning away [of the Reminder], so too is the end of your excess. In these three verses, as you see, there is a promise for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a threat for his people.

His saying, the Exalted: So We destroyed those who were more powerful than them, and the example of the ancients has passed (فَأَهْلَكْنا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ بَطْشاً وَمَضى مَثَلُ الْأَوَّلِينَ) [43:8]. Al-Raghib said: Power (البطش) is to seize something with force. End quote. In the verse, there is a shift (التفات) in His saying: "than them" (مِنْهُمْ) from address to the third person (الغيبة). The reason for this was to turn from addressing them to addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) because of their failure to take heed of these stories and lessons, and to serve as a preparation for His subsequent saying: "and the example of the ancients has passed." This is supported by His later saying: "And if you ask them," which is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The meaning of His saying: "and the example of the ancients has passed" is that the description of the ancient nations and how that which they used to mock encompassed them has already passed in the surahs revealed before this one.

His saying, the Exalted: If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘The All-mighty, the All-knowing created them’ (وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ خَلَقَهُنَّ الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ) [43:9]. In this verse and what follows it until the completion of six verses, there is an argument for His Lordship (ربوبيته) and His uniqueness therein, with an allusion to the Return (المعاد) and a rebuke for their excess. This is taken from their admission that He, the Exalted, is the Creator of all, then taking aspects of creation that are themselves the governance (تدبير) of the servants' affairs—such as making the earth a cradle for them, making paths therein, and sending down rain. This results in the conclusion that He, the Exalted, alone is the Owner and Governor of their affairs; thus, He is the Lord (الرب), and there is no lord besides Him.

By this, it becomes clear that the verse is an introduction and a preparation for the argument contained in the following verses. It has been mentioned repeatedly in this book that idolatry (الوثنية) does not deny that origination and creation (الصنع والإيجاد) belong to Him, the Exalted, alone; rather, it claims that the matter of governance (التدبير) belongs to others.

His saying, the Exalted: He who made the earth a cradle for you and made in it ways for you, so that you may be guided (الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مَهْداً وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ فِيها سُبُلاً لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ) [43:10]. That is, He made the earth such that you are nurtured in it just as children are nurtured in a cradle (المهد), and He made for you in the earth paths and ways that you traverse and by which you are guided to your destinations.

It is said: The meaning of "so that you may be guided" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ) is the hope that you may be guided to the knowledge of Allah and His oneness in worship (التوحيد في العبادة); however, the first meaning is more apparent.

In the speech, there is a shift (التفات) to addressing the people after the address had been turned away from them to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Perhaps the reason for this is to show special concern for this meaning in creation—namely, that governance is identical to creation. Thus, their admission that creation is exclusive to Allah, the Glorified, while claiming that governance belongs to others among His creation, is a contradiction in speech born of ignorance; so He rebuked them with this direct address.

His saying, the Exalted: and who sent down water from the sky in a measured amount, and We revived a dead land with it. Likewise you [too] shall be brought forth (وَالَّذِي نَزَّلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً بِقَدَرٍ فَأَنْشَرْنا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً كَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ) [43:11]. He qualified the sending down of water with "in a measured amount" (بِقَدَرٍ) to indicate that it is by will and governance, not by chance. "Revival" (الإنشار) is giving life. "Dead" (ميت) is a lightened form of "dead" (ميت) with a shaddah. Describing the "land" (البلدة) with it is because it is a place; for a land is only described with death and life insofar as it is a place. The shift from the third person to the first person plural (التكلم مع الغير) in "and We revived" (فَأَنْشَرْنا) is to show special concern.

When He argued by the sending down of water in a measured amount and the revival of the dead land for His creation and governance, He deduced from it another matter without which monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — is not complete: the Return (المعاد), which is the return of all to Him, the Exalted. Thus He said: Likewise you [too] shall be brought forth (كَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ); that is, just as He revived the dead land, so too shall you be resurrected from your graves alive.

It is said: In expressing the bringing forth of plants as "revival" (الإنشار)—which is the giving of life to the dead—and expressing their being given life as "bringing forth" (الإخراج), there is an exaltation of the matter of plant growth and a making easy of the matter of resurrection, in order to establish the methods of argumentation and clarify the path of analogy.

His saying, the Exalted: and who created all the pairs and made for you such of the ships and the cattle as you ride (وَالَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعامِ ما تَرْكَبُونَ) [43:12]. It is said: What is meant by "pairs" (الأزواج) are the categories of existing things, such as male and female, white and black, and others. It is also said: What is meant is the pair of everything; thus, everything other than Allah—such as above and below, right and left, male and female—is a pair.

His saying: and made for you such of the ships and the cattle as you ride (وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعامِ ما تَرْكَبُونَ); that is, you ride them. Riding (الركوب), when attributed to an animal like a horse or a camel, is transitive by itself; so it is said: "I rode the horse." When it is attributed to something like a ship or a vessel, it is transitive by "in" (في); so it is said: "He rode in it." The Exalted said: When they board the ship (فَإِذا رَكِبُوا فِي الْفُلْكِ). Thus, in His saying: "as you ride" (ما تَرْكَبُونَ), meaning "you ride them," the side of the cattle is given preponderance (تغليب).

His saying, the Exalted: that you may sit straight on their backs, then remember the blessing of your Lord when you are seated on them and say... until His saying ...shall surely return (لِتَسْتَوُوا عَلى ظُهُورِهِ ثُمَّ تَذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ إِذَا اسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَتَقُولُوا... لَمُنْقَلِبُونَ) [43:13-14]. Sitting straight (الاستواء) on the backs is to be firmly established upon them. The pronoun in "their backs" (ظُهُورِهِ) refers to the wording of the relative pronoun in "as you ride" (ما تَرْكَبُونَ). The pronoun in His saying: "when you are seated on them" (إِذَا اسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ) also refers to the relative pronoun; for just as it is said: "I sat straight on the back of the beast," it is said: "I sat straight on the beast."

What is meant by remembering the blessing of the Lord, the Glorified, after sitting straight on the backs of the ships and cattle, is the remembrance of the blessings by which man benefits through His, the Exalted, subjugation of these mounts for him—such as moving from place to place and carrying heavy loads. The Exalted said: and He has disposed the ships for you to sail on the sea by His command (إبراهيم: ٣٢), and He said: He created the cattle... until He said ...and they carry your loads to a town that you could not have reached except with great hardship to yourselves (النحل: ٧). Or what is meant is the remembrance of His blessings in general, by transitioning from the remembrance of these specific blessings to Him.

His saying: and say, ‘Immaculate is He who has disposed this for us, and we [by ourselves] were no match for it’ (وَتَقُولُوا سُبْحانَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَنا هذا وَما كُنَّا لَهُ مُقْرِنِينَ). That is, we were not capable of it; and being a match (الإقران) is capability.

The apparent meaning of remembering the blessing when using it and benefiting from it is to thank its Bestower. It follows from this that the remembrance of the blessing is other than saying: "Immaculate is He who..." (سُبْحانَ الَّذِي) etc. For this saying is a glorification (تسبيح) and a hallowing (تنزيه) of Him from that which does not befit the courtyard of His Greatness—namely, a partner in Lordship and Divinity. The remembrance of the blessing is gratitude (شكر)—as previously mentioned—and gratitude is other than hallowing.

This is supported by what has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام) regarding what is said when sitting straight on a mount; for the narrations, despite their differences, include praise (التحميد) alongside glorification, saying: "Immaculate is He who..." etc.

It is narrated in al-Kashshaf from al-Hasan ibn Ali (عليه‌السلام) that he saw a man mounting a beast and saying: "Immaculate is He who has disposed this for us." He said: "Is this what you were commanded?" The man asked: "And what were we commanded?" He replied: "That you remember the blessing of your Lord."

His saying: Indeed we shall surely return to our Lord (وَإِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا لَمُنْقَلِبُونَ). That is, we are going back; a testimony to the Return (المعاد).

Ayahs

وَجَعَلُوا۟ لَهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ جُزْءًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
They ascribe to Him offspring from among His servants! Man is indeed a manifest ingrate. (15)
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذَ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ بَنَاتٍ وَأَصْفَىٰكُم بِٱلْبَنِينَ (١٦)
Did He adopt daughters from what He creates while He preferred you with sons? (16)
وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِمَا ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مَثَلًا ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ (١٧)
When one of them is brought the news of what he ascribes to the All-beneficent, his face becomes darkened and he chokes with suppressed rage, [and says] (17)
أَوَمَن يُنَشَّؤُا۟ فِى ٱلْحِلْيَةِ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْخِصَامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ (١٨)
‘What! One who is brought up amid ornaments and is inconspicuous in contests?’ (18)
وَجَعَلُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبَـٰدُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ إِنَـٰثًا ۚ أَشَهِدُوا۟ خَلْقَهُمْ ۚ سَتُكْتَبُ شَهَـٰدَتُهُمْ وَيُسْـَٔلُونَ (١٩)
They have made the angels—who are servants of the All-beneficent—females. Were they witness to their creation? Their testimony will be written down and they shall be questioned. (19)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مَا عَبَدْنَـٰهُم ۗ مَّا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (٢٠)
They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’ They do not have any knowledge of that, and they do nothing but surmise. (20)
أَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ كِتَـٰبًا مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ فَهُم بِهِۦ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ (٢١)
Did We give them a Book before this, so that they are holding fast to it? (21)
بَلْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٢٢)
No, they said, ‘We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided in their footsteps.’ (22)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّذِيرٍ إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَآ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّقْتَدُونَ (٢٣)
So it has been that We did not send any warner to a town before you, without its affluent ones saying, ‘We found our fathers following a creed and we are indeed following in their footsteps.’ (23)
۞ قَـٰلَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُم بِأَهْدَىٰ مِمَّا وَجَدتُّمْ عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٢٤)
He would say, ‘What! Even if I bring you a better guidance than what you found your fathers following?!’ They would say, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you are sent with.’ (24)
فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٢٥)
Thereupon We took vengeance on them; so observe how was the fate of the deniers. (25)

(Commentary)

This section recounts some of their claims, to which they were driven by excess and ingratitude for blessings (الكفر بالنعم)—namely, their claim that Allah has offspring and that the angels are the daughters of Allah, the Glorified. It also addresses their arguments for worshipping the angels and provides a rebuttal against them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلُوا لَهُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ جُزْءاً إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَكَفُورٌ مُبِينٌ — "They attribute to Him a part of His servants. Indeed man is a manifest ingrate." (43:15). What is meant by "part" (جزءاً) is offspring, for procreation is nothing but derivation (الاشتقاق); thus, the offspring is a part of its parent, separated from him and fashioned after his form.

The offspring was expressed as a "part" (الجزء) to point toward the impossibility of their claim. This is because the partiality of one thing from another, however it is conceived, cannot be realized except through composition (التركب) within that thing, whereas Allah, the Glorified, is One (واحد) from every aspect.

It has become clear from what preceded that the phrase "of His servants" (مِنْ عِبادِهِ) serves as an exposition (بيان) for His saying "a part" (جزءاً). There is no harm in this type of exposition preceding the thing being expounded (المبين), nor in the exposition being plural while the thing being expounded is singular.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمِ اتَّخَذَ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ بَناتٍ وَأَصْفاكُمْ بِالْبَنِينَ — "Has He taken daughters from what He creates and favored you with sons?" (43:16). That is, He has reserved sons exclusively for you.

Thus, you have sons while He has nothing but daughters, even though you consider the daughter to be inferior to the son. You attribute to Him the inferior of the two categories while specializing yourselves with the more noble of them. This, in addition to being an impossible claim in itself, constitutes a manifest disparagement, insult, and ingratitude.

The restriction of "taking daughters" to being "from what He creates" (مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ) is because they claimed that the angels—despite their lordship and divinity—were created by Allah. The shift (الالتفات) in the verse to addressing them directly is to emphasize the obligation (الإلزام) and to establish the rebuke. The use of the indefinite for "daughters" (بنات) and the definite for "the sons" (البنين) is for the purpose of belittlement and aggrandizement, respectively.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُمْ بِما ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمنِ مَثَلاً ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ — "When one of them is brought the news of [the birth of] what he ascribes to the All-beneficent as a likeness, his face becomes darkened and he is choked with grief." (43:17). The likeness (المثل) is the peer and the similar thing that is of the same genus as something else. To "strike a likeness" (ضرب الشيء مثلاً) for something is to take it as being of the same genus. The phrase "of what he ascribes to the All-beneficent as a likeness" (بِما ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمنِ مَثَلاً) refers to the female. The grieved (الكظيم) is one filled with distress and rage.

The meaning is: Their state is such that when one of them is given the news of a female—whom he has made a similar peer to the All-Beneficent—his face turns black from sorrow, and he is filled with distress and rage because they do not accept that for themselves and consider it a disgrace, yet they accept it for Him.

The shift (الالتفات) in the verse to the third person (الغيبة) is to recount their hideous conduct and ugly way to others so that they may marvel at it.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَمَنْ يُنَشَّؤُا فِي الْحِلْيَةِ وَهُوَ فِي الْخِصامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ — "What! One who is brought up amid ornaments and who is non-demonstrative in disputes?" (43:18). That is, have they assigned to Allah, the Glorified, one who is brought up in ornaments (الحلية)—meaning raised in finery—and who, in disputes and argumentation, is unable to make their proof clear (غير مبين) or establish their claim?

These two attributes were mentioned because a woman, by nature, possesses stronger emotion and compassion and weaker intellect (التعقل) in comparison to a man, whereas the man is the opposite. Among the clearest manifestations of the strength of her emotions is her intense attachment to ornaments and finery, and her weakness in establishing an argument, which is based on the strength of intellect.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلُوا الْمَلائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ إِناثاً — "They have made the angels, who are servants of the All-beneficent, females..." (43:19). This is the meaning of their saying that the angels are the daughters of Allah. Groups among the pre-Islamic Arabs used to say this. As for other idolaters (الوثنية), they might have included among their deities a goddess who was the mother of a god or the daughter of a god, but they did not claim that all angels were females, as is apparent from what is recounted in the Noble Verse.

The angels were described by His saying: "who are servants of the All-beneficent" (الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ) as a rebuttal to the claim of their femininity, because the term "servants" (العباد) in this masculine form is not applied to females. This does not necessitate that they are characterized by masculinity (الذكورة) in the sense that an animal is characterized, for the masculinity and femininity found in animals are among the requirements of their material existence (الوجود المادي) equipped for procreation and the production of likenesses, whereas the angels are far removed from that.

His saying: أَشَهِدُوا خَلْقَهُمْ سَتُكْتَبُ شَهادَتُهُمْ وَيُسْئَلُونَ — "Did they witness their creation? Their testimony will be written down and they will be questioned." (43:19). This is a rebuttal of their claim of femininity in the angels by stating that the path to such knowledge is through the senses, yet they have not seen them to know this. They were not present at their creation to witness it.

Thus, His saying: "Did they witness their creation..." is a rhetorical question of denial (استفهام إنكاري) and a threat for speaking without knowledge. That is, they did not witness their creation, and this testimony of theirs will be written in the records of their deeds against them, and they will be questioned about it on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ — "They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’ They have no knowledge of that; they do nothing but conjecture." (43:20). This is a fallacious rational argument recounted from them, which can be formulated in two ways:

First, to establish the validity of worshipping partners (الشركاء) by saying: "If Allah had willed that we not worship the partners, we would not have worshipped them, by necessity, because it is impossible for His intended act to deviate from His will (إرادته). However, we do worship them; therefore, He did not will [that we stop], and the absence of His will for us to stop is an authorization (إذن) for our worship. Thus, there is no prohibition from Allah, the Exalted, against worshipping the partners, and the angels are among them." This meaning is what comes to mind from His saying in Surah al-An'am: سَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما أَشْرَكْنا وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "The polytheists will say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have ascribed any partner [to Him], nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything.’" (6:148), as indicated by the context before and after it.

Second, it can be formulated to invalidate prophethood (النبوة), which claims that Allah makes such-and-such obligatory upon you and forbids such-and-such. This is done by saying: "If Allah had willed that we not worship partners, nor permit or forbid anything, we would not have worshipped partners nor established any ruling from ourselves, because it is impossible for His intended act to deviate from His will. However, we do worship them and we do permit and forbid things; therefore, Allah, the Glorified, has not willed anything from us. Thus, the claim that Allah commands you with this and forbids you from that—and in short, that He willed such-and-such—is false."

This meaning is the apparent one understood from His saying, the Exalted, in Surah al-Nahl: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ نَحْنُ وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "The polytheists say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him—neither we nor our fathers—nor would we have forbidden anything besides Him.’" (16:35), considering the context.

Their saying in the recounted verse under discussion: "Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them" (لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ), according to what the context of the preceding and succeeding verses provides, is intended as an argument for the first meaning—which is the justification of their worship of the angels. Thus, it carries the same meaning as the verse in Surah al-An'am, though more specific.

His saying: "They have no knowledge of that" (ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ) means that this is a statement from them based on ignorance. It is a sophistry (مغالطة) in which they confused the creative will (الإرادة التكوينية) — the irresistible decree of Allah by which He brings things into existence — with the legislative will (الإرادة التشريعية) — the commands and prohibitions Allah sets for His servants to follow by their own choice — and took the former in place of the latter. The implication of the argument is only that there is no creative will from Him, the Exalted, pertaining to their non-worship of the angels. However, the absence of this type of will regarding their non-worship does not necessitate the absence of the legislative will regarding it.

Since He, the Glorified, did not will that they not worship partners through the creative will, they remained autonomous (مختارين), not compelled (غير مضطرين) to act or refrain. He then willed from them, through the legislative will, that they maintain monotheism (التوحيد) and not worship partners. It is not impossible for the intended act to deviate from the legislative will because it is conventional (اعتبارية), not ontological (حقيقية). It is only used in laws, regulations, and master-servant obligations (التكاليف المولوية), and the reality upon which it is based is the act's inclusion of a benefit (مصلحة) or a corruption (مفسدة).

From what has preceded, the flaw in the following claim becomes apparent: "Their argument is based on two premises: first, that their worship of the angels is by the will of Allah, the Exalted; and second, that this necessitates it being pleasing to Him. They were correct in the first and erred in the second, as they were ignorant that the will (المشية) consists of preferring some possibilities over others, whatever they may be, without considering pleasure or displeasure in either side."

The reason this is flawed is that the content of the argument is the absence of the will pertaining to the abandonment of worship. The absence of the will to abandon does not necessitate the presence of the will to act; rather, its consequence is permission (الإذن), which is the absence of prohibition from the act. Furthermore, the proponent of this view restricted "will" to the creative and neglected the legislative, upon which master-servant obligations revolve, and this is an error on his part.

Also apparent is the flaw in what has been attributed to some: that the meaning of their saying, "Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them," is an excuse for worshipping the angels by attributing it to Allah's will, while admitting it is ugly.

This is because they did not concede the ugliness of worshipping their deities such that they would offer an excuse for it. Indeed, it is recounted from them later: "We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided by their footsteps."

His saying: "they do nothing but conjecture" (إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ). Conjecture (الخرص)—according to what appears from al-Raghib—is speaking based on opinion and guesswork; it has also been interpreted as lying.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ آتَيْناهُمْ كِتاباً مِنْ قَبْلِهِ فَهُمْ بِهِ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ — "Have We given them a Book before this, so that they are holding fast to it?" (43:21). The pronoun in "before this" (مِنْ قَبْلِهِ) refers to the Quran. The verse denies that they have any proof from the path of transmission (النقل), just as the previous verse denied their proof from the path of reason (العقل). The sum of the two verses is that they have no proof for worshipping the angels, neither through reason nor through transmission; thus, Allah has not authorized it.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ قالُوا إِنَّا وَجَدْنا آباءَنا عَلى أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلى آثارِهِمْ مُهْتَدُونَ — "Rather they say, ‘We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided by their footsteps.’" (43:22). The creed (الأمة) is the way that is aimed for and intended; what is meant by it is religion (الدين). This is a digression (إضراب) from what was concluded by the two verses. The meaning is: They have no evidence for the truth of their worship; rather, they said, "We found our fathers following a religion and we are guided by their footsteps." That is, they are merely clinging to the imitation (تقليد) of their fathers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ ما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ إِلَّا قالَ مُتْرَفُوها إِنَّا وَجَدْنا — "And so it has been that We did not send any warner to a town before you but its affluent ones said, ‘We found...’" (43:23). That is, clinging to the hem of imitation is not something exclusive to these people; rather, it was the habit of their predecessors among the polytheistic nations. We did not send before you to any town a warner (نذير)—who is the Prophet—but that its people of luxury and ease clung to the hem of imitation and said: "We found our predecessors following a religion, and we are following in their footsteps; we shall not abandon it nor oppose them."

The attribution of this statement to their "affluent ones" (مترفيهم) is to indicate that luxury and ease are what drive them toward imitation and divert them from contemplating the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِأَهْدى مِمَّا وَجَدْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ آباءَكُمْ — "He said, ‘What! Even if I bring you a better guidance than what you found your fathers following?’" (43:24). The speaker is the warner, and the address is to the affluent ones, including others by extension. The conjunction in "What! Even if I bring you" (أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُمْ) is linked to a deleted phrase indicated by their speech. The estimation is: "Are you following in their footsteps even if I bring you a better guidance than what you found your fathers following?" The warner's description of what he brought them as "better guidance" (أهدى) than their religion—despite their religion being false with no guidance in it—is a matter of engaging the opponent on their own terms (مجاراة الخصم).

And their saying: قالُوا إِنَّا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ كافِرُونَ — "They said, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you have been sent with.’" (43:24). This is their response to the warner's saying: "What! Even if I bring you...", and it is an arbitrary assertion (تحكم) without proof.

His saying, the Exalted: فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ — "So We took vengeance on them. So observe how was the end of the deniers!" (43:25). That is, as a consequence of that sending [of messengers] and the rejection through imitation and arbitrary assertion, We destroyed them for their denial. So look at how the end was for those predecessors among the people of the towns. In this is a threat to the people of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّنِى بَرَآءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ (٢٦)
When Abraham said to his father and his people, ‘I repudiate what you worship, (26)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِى فَإِنَّهُۥ سَيَهْدِينِ (٢٧)
excepting Him who originated me; indeed He will guide me.’ (27)
وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةًۢ بَاقِيَةً فِى عَقِبِهِۦ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٢٨)
He made it a lasting word among his posterity so that they may come back [to the right path]. (28)
بَلْ مَتَّعْتُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ وَرَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢٩)
Indeed, I provided for these and their fathers until the truth and a manifest apostle came to them. (29)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ وَإِنَّا بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٠)
But when the truth came to them, they said, ‘This is magic, and we indeed disbelieve in it.’ (30)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنَ ٱلْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ (٣١)
And they said, ‘Why was not this Quran sent down to some great man from the two cities?’ (31)
أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ ۚ نَحْنُ قَسَمْنَا بَيْنَهُم مَّعِيشَتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَرَفَعْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَـٰتٍ لِّيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُم بَعْضًا سُخْرِيًّا ۗ وَرَحْمَتُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ (٣٢)
Is it they who dispense the mercy of your Lord? It is We who have dispensed among them their livelihood in the present life, and raised some of them above others in rank, so that some may take others into service, and your Lord’s mercy is better than what they amass. (32)
وَلَوْلَآ أَن يَكُونَ ٱلنَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً لَّجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَمَعَارِجَ عَلَيْهَا يَظْهَرُونَ (٣٣)
Were it not [for the danger] that mankind would be one community, We would have made for those who defy the All-beneficent, silver roofs for their houses and [silver] stairways by which they ascend, (33)
وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْوَٰبًا وَسُرُرًا عَلَيْهَا يَتَّكِـُٔونَ (٣٤)
and [silver] doors for their houses and [silver] couches on which they recline, (34)
وَزُخْرُفًا ۚ وَإِن كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣٥)
and ornaments of gold; yet all that would be nothing but the wares of the life of this world, and the Hereafter is for the Godwary near your Lord. (35)
وَمَن يَعْشُ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُۥ شَيْطَـٰنًا فَهُوَ لَهُۥ قَرِينٌ (٣٦)
We assign a devil to be the companion of him who turns a blind eye to the remembrance of the All-beneficent. (36)
وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٣٧)
Indeed they bar them from the way [of Allah], while they suppose that they are [rightly] guided. (37)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَنَا قَالَ يَـٰلَيْتَ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرِينُ (٣٨)
When he comes to Us, he will say, ‘I wish there had been between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion [you are]!’ (38)
وَلَن يَنفَعَكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ إِذ ظَّلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ (٣٩)
‘Today that will be of no avail to you. As you did wrong, so will you share in the punishment.’ (39)
أَفَأَنتَ تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ أَوْ تَهْدِى ٱلْعُمْىَ وَمَن كَانَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٤٠)
Can you, then, make the deaf hear or guide the blind and those who are in manifest error? (40)
فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ فَإِنَّا مِنْهُم مُّنتَقِمُونَ (٤١)
We will indeed take vengeance on them, whether We take you away (41)
أَوْ نُرِيَنَّكَ ٱلَّذِى وَعَدْنَـٰهُمْ فَإِنَّا عَلَيْهِم مُّقْتَدِرُونَ (٤٢)
or show you what We have promised them, for indeed We hold them in Our power. (42)
فَٱسْتَمْسِكْ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٤٣)
So hold fast to what has been revealed to you. Indeed, you are on a straight path. (43)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَذِكْرٌ لَّكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ ۖ وَسَوْفَ تُسْـَٔلُونَ (٤٤)
Indeed it is a reminder for you and your people, and soon you will be questioned. (44)
وَسْـَٔلْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رُّسُلِنَآ أَجَعَلْنَا مِن دُونِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ءَالِهَةً يُعْبَدُونَ (٤٥)
Ask those of Our apostles We have sent before you: Did We set up any gods to be worshipped besides the All-beneficent? (45)

(Commentary)

When the discourse led to their rejection of the Messenger's message and their disbelief in it out of mere stubbornness, and their clinging to polytheism (الشرك) — the association of partners with Allah — by following the tail of blind imitation (تقليد) of their fathers and ancestors without proof, He followed that by referring to the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and his rejection of the imitation of his father and his people, his disavowal of what they worshipped besides Allah, the Glorified, and his seeking the guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — of his Lord who originated him.

Then He mentions His granting them enjoyment of His blessings and their ingratitude toward them by disbelieving in the Book of Allah and their slandering it and His Messenger with that which is refuted back upon them. Then He mentions the consequence of turning away from the remembrance (ذكر) of Allah and the misery and loss to which it leads. He follows this by mentioning the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) despair regarding their faith and threatening them with punishment. He emphasizes the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) to hold fast to the Quran, and that it is indeed a reminder (ذكر) for him and for his people, and they shall soon be questioned about it; and that the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — contained within it is the same that the prophets before him adhered to.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ إِنَّنِي بَراءٌ مِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ "When Abraham said to his father and his people, ‘I am indeed quit of what you worship." (43:26). The word al-bara’ (البراء) is a verbal noun (مصدر) from bari’a yabrau (برىء يبرأ), meaning he is "quit" or "disassociated" (بريء). Thus, the meaning of "I am indeed bara’" (إِنَّنِي بَراءٌ) is "I am a possessor of disavowal" or "I am quit" by way of hyperbole (مبالغة), similar to saying "Zayd is justice" (زيد عدل).

In the verse, there is an indication of Ibrahim's (عليه السلام) disavowal of the idols and stars that his father and his people used to worship after he had argued with them regarding them, yet they relied upon the practice of their fathers, as mentioned in the Surahs of al-An'am, al-Anbiya, al-Shu'ara, and others.

The meaning is: And mention to them when Ibrahim disavowed the gods of his father and his people, as they were worshipping them out of imitation (تقليد) of their fathers without any proof (حجة), while he stood alone in rational contemplation (النظر).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي فَإِنَّهُ سَيَهْدِينِ "except Him who originated me, and He will surely guide me." (43:27). That is, except the One who brought me into existence (أوجدني), and He is Allah, the Glorified. In His description as the Originator (الفطر), there is an indication of the proof for His Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية), for origination and bringing into existence are inseparable from the governance (تدبير) of the affairs of the originated being. Thus, He who originated the whole is the One who governs their affairs; therefore, He is the One worthy of being worshipped.

His saying: فَإِنَّهُ سَيَهْدِينِ "and He will surely guide me" means to the truth that I seek. It is also said: to the path of Paradise. In this sentence, there is an indication of another characteristic of Lordship (ربوبية), which is guidance (الهداية) to the true path that man must traverse. For leading toward perfection (الكمال) is part of the completeness of governance (التدبير). Thus, it is incumbent upon the Lord who governs the affairs of His subject to guide him to his perfection and felicity (سعادة). The Exalted said: رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى "Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it." (20:50), and He said: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ "With Allah rests the [guidance to the] straight path" (16:9). Thus, returning to Allah through the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة) entails guidance, as the Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways" (29:69).

The exception in His saying: إِلَّا الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي "except Him who originated me" is a disconnected exception (منقطع) because the idolaters (الوثنيين) do not worship Allah, as has been mentioned repeatedly. Thus, the claim of some that it is a connected exception (متصل) and that they used to say "Allah is our Lord" while worshipping idols is as you see [unfounded].

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلَها كَلِمَةً باقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ "He made it a lasting word among his posterity, so that they may come back." (43:28). It is apparent that the hidden subject pronoun in "He made it" (جَعَلَها) refers to Allah, the Glorified. The attached object pronoun — according to what has been said — refers to the "word of disavowal" (كلمة البراءة) spoken by Ibrahim (عليه السلام). Its meaning is the meaning of the word of monotheism (كلمة التوحيد), for the import of "There is no god but Allah" (لا إله إلا الله) is the negation of gods other than Allah, not the negation of gods and then the affirmation of the Deity, the Exalted (1). This is apparent, so there is no need for the strained interpretation of some that the pronoun refers to the "word of monotheism" known from what Ibrahim (عليه السلام) spoke.

What is meant by "his posterity" (عقبه) is his progeny and children. His saying: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ "so that they may come back" means they return from the worship of gods other than Allah to His worship, the Exalted. That is, some of them — those who worship other than Allah — return through the invitation of others among them — those who worship Allah — to His worship. Through this, it becomes clear that the meaning of the "lasting" of the word in his posterity is that they shall not be devoid of a monotheist (موحد) as long as they exist. Perhaps this is due to the answering of his (عليه السلام) prayer when he said: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ "and keep me and my sons away from worshipping idols." (14:35).

It is also said: the pronoun in "He made" (جعل) refers to Ibrahim (عليه السلام); thus, he is the one who made this word lasting in his posterity, hoping they would return to it. What is meant by his making it lasting among them is his testament (وصيته) to them regarding it, as the Exalted said: وَصَّى بِها إِبْراهِيمُ بَنِيهِ وَيَعْقُوبُ يا بَنِيَّ إِنَّ اللهَ اصْطَفى لَكُمُ الدِّينَ فَلا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ "And Abraham enjoined this upon his sons, and [so did] Jacob, ‘O my sons! Allah has indeed chosen this religion for you; so do not die except as Muslims.’" (2:132).

However, you are aware that the testament (الوصية) regarding the word of monotheism is not called "making the word lasting in the posterity," even if it is correct to say he intended that by it; but it is not the same as "making it lasting" among them.

It is also said: the meaning is that Allah made the Imamate (الإمامة) — the position of divine leadership over the community — a lasting word in his posterity. The discussion regarding this will come in the forthcoming "Tradition-based Discourse," Allah willing.

It appears from the verse that the progeny of Ibrahim (عليه السلام) will not be devoid of this word until the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ مَتَّعْتُ هؤُلاءِ وَآباءَهُمْ حَتَّى جاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ وَرَسُولٌ مُبِينٌ "Nay, I let these and their fathers enjoy until the truth came to them and a manifest messenger." (43:29). This is a digression (إضراب) from what is understood from the previous verse. The meaning is: their return from polytheism to monotheism was the hoped-for goal for them, but they did not return; rather, I let these people of your folk and their fathers enjoy, so they took pleasure in My blessings "until the truth came to them and a manifest messenger."

Perhaps the shift (الالتفات) to the first-person singular in His saying: "Nay, I let [them] enjoy" (بَلْ مَتَّعْتُ) is to indicate the gravity of their crime, and that in their ingratitude for the blessing and their disbelief in the truth and their labeling it as sorcery, they intend none but Him, the Exalted, alone.

What is meant by "the truth" (الحق) that came to them is the Quran, and by "the manifest messenger" (الرسول المبين) is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ قالُوا هذا سِحْرٌ وَإِنَّا بِهِ كافِرُونَ "But when the truth came to them, they said, ‘This is magic, and we indeed disbelieve in it.’" (43:30). This is their slander against the truth that came to them, which is the Quran, and it necessitates slandering the Messenger. Just as their forthcoming saying: "Why was [it] not sent down..." etc., is the same.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ عَلى رَجُلٍ مِنَ الْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ "And they said, ‘Why was this Quran not sent down to some great man from the two cities?’" (43:31). What is meant by "the two cities" (القريتين) is Mecca and Ta'if. Their meaning of "greatness" (العظمة) — as the context suggests — is that which pertains to wealth and status (الجاه), which are the criteria for nobility (الشرافة) and high rank among the children of this world. The meaning of His saying: "a great man from the two cities" (رَجُلٍ مِنَ الْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ) is a man from one of the two cities, where the governing noun (المضاف) was omitted for brevity (إيجاز).

Their intent was that the messengership (الرسالة) is a noble divine station that should not be assumed except by a man who is noble in himself, great and obeyed among his people. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) was poor and lacked this trait. Thus, if the Quran he brought were a revelation (وحي) sent down from Allah, why was it not sent down to a great man from Mecca or Ta'if, possessing much wealth and high status?

In al-Majma': "They mean by the 'great man from one of the two cities' al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah from Mecca and Abu Mas'ud 'Urwah ibn Mas'ud al-Thaqafi from Ta'if, narrated from Qatadah. It is also said: 'Utbah ibn Rabi'ah from Mecca and Ibn 'Abd Yalil from Ta'if, narrated from Mujahid. And it is said: al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah from Mecca and Habib ibn 'Umar al-Thaqafi from Ta'if, narrated from Ibn 'Abbas." End quote.

The truth is that these are applications (تطبيق) by the commentators; they only spoke in general terms, intending one of these magnates of the two cities, as is apparent from the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ نَحْنُ قَسَمْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا "Is it they who dispense the mercy of your Lord? It is We who have dispensed among them their livelihood in the present life..." (43:32) etc. What is meant by "mercy" (الرحمة) — as the context indicates — is prophethood (النبوة).

Al-Raghib said: Livelihood (العيش) is the life specific to animals; it is more specific than "life" (الحياة) because life is said of animals, the Creator (الباري), and the angels. From it is derived ma'ishah (المعيشة) for that by which one lives. End quote. He also said: Subjugation (التسخير) is driving something toward a specific purpose by force — until he said: and Sukhri (السخري) is the one who is overpowered and thus subjugated by his own will. End quote.

The verse and the two verses after it are in the position of answering their saying: "Why was this Quran not sent down to some [great] man..." etc. Their summary is that this saying of theirs is a manifest stubbornness that should be wondered at, for they are passing judgment on what they do not possess. This livelihood of theirs in the life of this world, by which they live and receive sustenance, is a mercy from Us that has no value or status with Us; it is nothing but a vanishing enjoyment. We dispense it among them, and it is outside their power and will. So how can they dispense prophethood, which is the greatest mercy and the key to the permanent felicity of mankind and eternal success, giving it to whom they wish and withholding it from whom they wish?

Thus, His saying: أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ "Is it they who dispense the mercy of your Lord?" is an interrogative of denial (الإنكار). The shift to the third person in His saying: "the mercy of your Lord" (رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ), rather than saying "Our mercy," is to indicate the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) exclusivity in the care of Lordship regarding prophethood.

The meaning is: they do not possess prophethood, which is a mercy of Allah specific to Him, such that they could withhold it from you and give it to whom they desire.

His saying: نَحْنُ قَسَمْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا "It is We who have dispensed among them their livelihood in the present life" is an explanation of the reason for the denial in the previous sentence: that they are incapable of dispensing what is many degrees below prophethood and has no status, which is their livelihood in the life of this world. We have dispensed it among them, so how can they dispense what is of a higher status by an immeasurable degree, namely prophethood, which is the specific mercy of your Lord?

The proof that provisions (الأرزاق) and livelihoods are not in the hand of man is the variation among individuals in wealth and poverty, well-being and health, in children and other things counted as provision. Everyone desires to acquire the maximum of these, yet it is hardly possible for any of them to attain all that he hopes for and is pleased with. If that were in the hand of man, no one would be found destitute or poor in anything; rather, no two people would differ in it. Thus, their variation in it is the clearest proof that provision is dispensed by a will from Allah, not by man.

Furthermore, the will and action of man are only some of the partial causes (الأسباب الناقصة) for attaining the objective, which is provision. Beyond them are countless cosmic causes (أسباب كونية) outside the power of man; the objective is not attained except by the occurrence of all of them and their convergence upon it, and they are in none but the hand of Allah, to Whom all causes return.

All of this pertains to wealth. As for status (الجاه), it is also dispensed by Allah, for it depends on specific qualities by which the degrees of a person are raised in society, enabling him to subjugate those below him — such as intelligence, shrewdness, courage, high ambition, firm resolve, abundance of wealth and kin. None of that is completed except by the making of Allah, the Glorified. That is His saying: وَرَفَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجاتٍ لِيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضاً سُخْرِيًّا "and raised some of them above others in rank, so that some may take others into service." (43:32).

Thus, it becomes clear through the combination of the two statements — His saying: "We have dispensed..." and His saying: "and We raised some of them above others..." — that the dispenser of livelihood and status among people is Allah, the Glorified, and none other. His saying: وَرَحْمَتُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ مِمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ "and the mercy of your Lord is better than what they amass" means prophethood is better than wealth; so how can they possess its dispensation when they do not possess the dispensation of wealth among themselves?

It is possible that His saying: "and We raised some of them above others" is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) to His saying: "We have dispensed among them their livelihood," explaining the dispensation of livelihood among them by explaining the causes of its division in human society. The explanation of this is that the abundance of man's needs in his worldly life is such that he cannot fulfill all of them in an isolated life. This necessitated his joining with other individuals, first by way of employment and seeking benefit, and second by way of cooperation and mutual support, as mentioned in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume of this book.

So the matter led to a general exchange (المعاوضة العامة) that results in a type of specialization (الاختصاص), such that each gives from what he has of life's needs that which exceeds his own need, and takes in return from another what is equivalent to it of what he needs. For example, he gives the excess water he has acquired and specialized in, and takes from another the excess food beyond the other's strength. The necessity of this is that every individual strives in what he is predisposed for and excels at, thereby acquiring what he needs and specializes in. The necessity of that is that others need him for the goods of life he possesses, so they are subjugated (يتسخر) to him, and he provides them with what they need. For instance, the baker needs the water the water-carrier has, and vice versa; so they cooperate through exchange. Similarly, the master is subjugated to the servant for his service, and the servant is subjugated to the master for his wealth, and so on. Thus, every part of society is subjugated to others by what he possesses, and others are subjugated to him, either directly, or through one or multiple intermediaries, because everyone rises above others by what he specializes in of what he possesses, in degrees that vary according to the variation of interests and purposes.

Based on the foregoing, what is meant by "livelihood" (المعيشة) is everything by which one lives, more general than wealth and status, or specifically wealth, with other things following it, as supported by His saying at the end: "and the mercy of your Lord is better than what they amass," for what is meant by it is wealth, and other necessities of life are intended by implication.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً — until His saying — وَمَعارِجَ عَلَيْها يَظْهَرُونَ "Were it not that mankind would have become a single community, [We would have provided for those who disbelieve in the All-beneficent, for their houses, roofs of silver, and stairs [of silver] by which they ascend]" (43:33). This verse and what follows it are to explain that the enjoyment of the world, in terms of wealth and finery (زينة), has no value or status with Allah, the Glorified.

They said: what is meant by people being "a single community" (أمة واحدة) is their being united upon a single way (سنة), which is disbelief (الكفر) in Allah, if they were to see that the finery of the world in its entirety was with the disbeliever in Allah, while the believer was absolutely empty-handed of it. "Stairs" (المعارج) are degrees and climbing places.

The meaning is: were it not that people would unite upon disbelief if they saw the luxury of the disbelievers and the deprivation of the believers, We would have made for those who disbelieve in the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) roofs of silver for their houses, and degrees by which they appear above others.

It is also possible that what is meant by people being "a single community" is their all being on the same footing regarding the causes (الأسباب) acting upon the fortunes of life, without distinction between the believer and the disbeliever. Thus, whoever strives his striving for provision and is favored by other connecting causes and factors attains it, whether he be a believer or a disbeliever; and whoever does not have them converge for him is deprived of it and provision is restricted for him, whether he be a believer or a disbeliever.

The meaning is: were it not for Our will that people be equal regarding the causes leading to the ornaments of the world, and that they not differ in them based on faith and disbelief, We would have made for those who disbelieve, etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْواباً وَسُرُراً عَلَيْها يَتَّكِؤُنَ وَزُخْرُفاً "and [silver] doors for their houses and [silver] couches on which they recline, and ornaments." (43:34-35). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "doors" (أَبْواباً) and "couches" (سُرُراً) is for magnification (التفخيم). Ornament (الزخرف) is gold or finery in general. It is said in al-Majma': "Ornament (الزخرف) is the perfection of a thing's beauty; from it, gold was so called. It is said: he ornamented it (زخرفه) when he beautified and decorated it. From this, engravings and images are called zukhruf. In the hadith: he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) did not enter the Kaaba until he ordered the zukhruf (ornamentation/images) to be removed." End quote. The rest is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كُلُّ ذلِكَ لَمَّا مَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةُ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ "Yet all these are nothing but the wares of the life of this world, and the Hereafter with your Lord is for the God-conscious." (43:35). The word in (إِنْ) is for negation and lamma (لَمَّا) means "except"; i.e., all that has been mentioned of the advantages of livelihood is nothing but the enjoyment of the life of this world, which is vanishing, ephemeral, and does not last.

His saying: وَالْآخِرَةُ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ "and the Hereafter with your Lord is for the God-conscious." What is meant by "the Hereafter" (الآخرة), by the context of the situation, is the felicitous life of the Hereafter, as if the wretched life of the Hereafter is not considered a life.

The meaning is: the felicitous life of the Hereafter, by a decree and judgment from Allah, is exclusive to the God-conscious (المتقين). This specification and restriction (القصر) somewhat supports the meaning we previously offered for people being "a single community" in the world.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطاناً فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ "We assign a devil to be the companion of him who turns a blind eye to the remembrance of the All-beneficent." (43:36). It is said: 'ashiya ya'sha 'ashan (عشي يعشى عشا) from the pattern of 'alima ya'lamu when there is an ailment in his sight such that he cannot see at all or only at night. And 'asha ya'shu 'ashwan (عشا يعشو عشوا) from the pattern of nasara yansuru when he feigns blindness or acts as if he cannot see without an ailment. Assigning (التقييض) is the decreeing and bringing of one thing to another; it is said: He assigned it to him when He brought it to him.

When the discourse concluded with the mention of the God-conscious and that the Hereafter is for them with Allah, He paired it with the end of the affair of those who turn away from the truth, acting blindly toward the remembrance (ذكر) of the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), indicating their affair from its beginning: that their acting blindly toward the remembrance of Allah causes them to be accompanied by the companions (قرناء) of the devils, who stick to them, misguiding them until they enter the punishment of the Hereafter with them.

Thus, His saying: وَمَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطاناً "We assign a devil to... him who turns a blind eye to the remembrance of the All-beneficent" means: whoever acts blindly toward the remembrance of the All-Beneficent and looks at it with the sight of one who cannot see clearly, We bring to him a devil. He, the Exalted, expressed this in another place as "sending," saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا الشَّياطِينَ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ تَؤُزُّهُمْ أَزًّا "Have you not regarded that We have sent the devils against the faithless to egg them on [to sin]?" (19:83). The attribution of "remembrance" (الذكر) to "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) is to indicate that it is a mercy.

His saying: فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ "to be [his] companion" means a companion who does not leave him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ "Indeed they bar them from the way, and they think they are rightly guided." (43:37). The pronoun "they" (أَنَّهُمْ) refers to the devils, and the remaining plural pronouns refer to those who act blindly toward the remembrance, considering the plural based on the meaning of "whoever acts blindly" (وَمَنْ يَعْشُ) etc. Barring (الصد) is turning away. What is meant by "the way" (السبيل) is that which the remembrance calls to: the way of Allah, which is the religion of monotheism.

The meaning is: the devils indeed turn those who act blindly toward the remembrance away, while those who act blindly think that they — i.e., the blind ones themselves — are guided to the truth.

This — namely, their thinking they are guided while being barred from the way of truth — is a sign of the assignment of the companion and their entering under the guardianship (ولاية) of the devil. For man, by his primary nature (الفطرة), is disposed toward inclining to the truth and recognizing it when it is presented to him. Then, if it is presented to him and he turns away from it following his desire (الهوى) and persists in it, Allah seals his heart, blinds his sight, and assigns to him the companion. Thus, he does not see the truth that appeared to him, and he applies the truth to which he inclines by nature to the falsehood to which the devil calls him; so he thinks he is guided while he is astray, and it is imagined to him that he is upon the truth while he is upon falsehood.

This is the "veil" (الغطاء) that He, the Exalted, mentions is cast over them in the world, and which will be uncovered from them on the Day of Resurrection. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ كانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِي غِطاءٍ عَنْ ذِكْرِي — until He said — قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمالاً الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعاً "those whose eyes were covered from My remembrance... Say, ‘Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works? Those whose endeavors go awry in the life of the world, while they suppose they are doing good.’" (18:101-104). And He said regarding what He addresses him with on the Day of Resurrection while his companion is with him: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — until He said — قالَ قَرِينُهُ رَبَّنا ما أَطْغَيْتُهُ وَلكِنْ كانَ فِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ "‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have suspended your veil from you, and your sight is sharp today.’ ... His companion will say, ‘Our Lord! I did not make him rebel, but he was [himself] in extreme error.’" (50:22-27).

His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَنا قالَ يا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ "When he comes to Us, he will say, ‘I wish there were between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion!’" (43:38). The word hatta (حَتَّى) is the limit (غاية) for the continuation of the action indicated by His saying in the previous verse: "they bar them" (لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ) and His saying: "they think" (يَحْسَبُونَ). That is, the companions continue to bar them and they continue to think they are guided until one of them comes to Us.

What is meant by "coming to Us" is the Resurrection (البعث). The pronouns in "he comes" (جاءَ) and "he says" (قالَ) refer to the relative pronoun [whoever] considering its singular form. What is meant by "the two easts" (المشرقين) is the east and the west, where the side of the east was made dominant in the dual form.

The meaning is: they continue in their barring from the way, and those who act blindly toward the remembrance continue in their thinking that they are guided in their being barred, until one of them is present before Us with his companion, and his misguidance and the painful punishment it entails are uncovered for him. He says, addressing his companion while suffering from his company: "I wish there were between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion you are!"

It is understood from the context that they are punished by the company of the companions in addition to their punishment by the Fire; therefore, they wish for distance from them and mention it specifically, forgetting other punishments.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَنْ يَنْفَعَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ إِذْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِي الْعَذابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ "It will not avail you today, as you were unjust, that you are partners in the punishment." (43:39). It is apparent that this is conjoined to what precedes it of the description of their state. What is meant by "today" (اليوم) is the Day of Resurrection. His saying: "that you are partners in the punishment" (أَنَّكُمْ فِي الْعَذابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ) is the subject of "it will not avail you" (لَنْ يَنْفَعَكُمُ). What is meant by the second-person plural pronoun are those who act blindly toward the remembrance and their companions. "As you were unjust" (إِذْ ظَلَمْتُمْ) stands in the place of a causal explanation (التعليل).

The meaning — and Allah knows best — is: when some of you wronged others in the world and caused them to fall into a calamity, you might have found some consolation (تسلي) if he himself were afflicted with the same as what he afflicted you with; so that would avail you as consolation and healing of the heart. However, it will not avail you on the Day of Resurrection that your companions share with you in the punishment, for their sharing with you in the punishment and their being with you in the Fire is itself a punishment for you.

Some commentators mentioned that the subject of "it will not avail you" is a pronoun referring to their wish mentioned in the previous verse. His saying: "as you were unjust" means because of your wronging yourselves in the world by following them in disbelief and sins. His saying: "that you are partners in the punishment" is a causal explanation for the negation of benefit. The meaning: the wish for distance will not avail you because it is your right that you and your companions share in the punishment.

In this, there is a contradiction, for he took His saying: "as you were unjust" as a causal explanation for the negation of the wish's benefit first, and His saying: "that you are partners in the punishment" as a second causal explanation for it. The necessity of the correspondence between the two explanations is that the second should mention the decree of punishment upon the wishing followers, not the sharing of the followers and the followed in it.

Some said: the meaning of the verse is that sharing does not lighten any of the punishment for you, because each one of you and your companions has the fullest share of punishment.

In this is what was said of the previous: that what was mentioned as the reason for the lack of benefit, even if assumed correct in itself, has no indication from the wording of the verse or the context of the discourse.

Some said: the meaning is: your sharing in the punishment does not avail you as the sharing of those who fall into the hardships of the world avails them, due to their cooperation in bearing its burdens and their dividing its toil; because each of you and your companions has a punishment that his capacity cannot reach.

In this is what was in the one before it. It was also refuted that benefiting in that way is not something that would occur to their minds such that it would be refuted by its negation.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَأَنْتَ تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ أَوْ تَهْدِي الْعُمْيَ وَمَنْ كانَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ "Can you then make the deaf hear, or guide the blind and him who is in manifest error?" (43:40). When He mentioned His assigning the companions to them and their turning their perception such that they see misguidance as guidance and are unable to recognize the truth, He branched from it by alerting him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) that these are deaf and blind; he is not able to make them hear the word of truth or guide them to the path of rectitude. So he should not exert himself or burden himself in inviting them, nor should he grieve for their turning away. The interrogation is for denial, and the rest is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ فَإِنَّا مِنْهُمْ مُنْتَقِمُونَ أَوْ نُرِيَنَّكَ الَّذِي وَعَدْناهُمْ فَإِنَّا عَلَيْهِمْ مُقْتَدِرُونَ "Whether We take you away, We shall indeed take vengeance on them, or We show you what We have promised them, for We have full power over them." (43:41-42). What is meant by "taking you away" (نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ) is taking him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) in death before taking vengeance on them. It is also said: it means taking him away by removing him from among them. His saying: "We shall indeed take vengeance on them" means inevitably. What is meant by "showing you what We have promised them" is taking vengeance on them before his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) death or while he is among them. His saying: "for We have full power over them" means Our power surpasses them.

His saying at the beginning: "Whether We take you away" (فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ) is originally in nadhhab bika (إن نذهب بك), to which ma (ما) was added and the nun (ن) for emphasis. The summary of the verse is: We shall take vengeance on them after your death or before it, inevitably.

His saying, the Exalted: فاسْتَمْسِكْ بِالَّذِي أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ إِنَّكَ عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "So hold fast to what has been revealed to you; indeed you are on a straight path." (43:43). It is apparent that this is a branching (تفريع) from all that preceded: that the sending down of the remembrance (الذكر) by way of revelation (الوحي) and prophethood (النبوة) is among His, the Exalted, ways (سنن); and that His Book sent down to him is truth, and he is a manifest messenger; none respond to his call except the God-conscious, and none turn away from it except the companions of the devils; and there is no hope in their faith, and Allah will soon take vengeance on them.

So He emphasized the command to him after all that, to be diligent in holding fast to the Book that was revealed to him, because he is on a straight path (صراط مستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ وَسَوْفَ تُسْئَلُونَ "It is indeed a reminder for you and for your people, and you will be questioned." (43:44). It is apparent that what is meant by "reminder" (الذكر) is the remembrance of Allah; in this sense, it has been repeated several times in the Surah. The lam (ل) in "for you and for your people" (لَكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ) is for specification (الاختصاص), in the sense of the direction of the obligations (التكاليف) within it toward them.
