This is somewhat supported by His saying: "and you will be questioned" (وَسوفَ تُسْئَلُونَ), i.e., about it on the Day of Resurrection.

According to most commentators, what is meant by "reminder" (الذكر) is the honor (الشرف) by which one is remembered. The meaning: it is indeed a great honor for you and for your people among the Arabs, by which you shall be remembered among the nations.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسْئَلْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رُسُلِنا أَجَعَلْنا مِنْ دُونِ الرَّحْمنِ آلِهَةً يُعْبَدُونَ "Ask those of Our messengers whom We sent before you: ‘Did We appoint any gods to be worshipped besides the All-beneficent?’" (43:45). It is said: what is meant by "asking them" is asking their nations and the scholars of their religion, like His saying, the Exalted: فَسْئَلِ الَّذِينَ يَقْرَؤُنَ الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ "ask those who read the Book [revealed] before you." (9:94). The benefit of this metaphor (المجاز) is that the matter asked about from them is the very thing their messengers brought, not what they answer of their own accord.

It is also said: what is meant is asking the People of the Two Books: the Torah and the Gospel. For even if they disbelieved, the proof is established by the mass-transmission (تواتر) of their report. The address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) and the obligation is for his community.

The distance [from the truth] of these two views is not hidden, and the second is further distanced by its specification of the People of the Two Books without a manifest specifier.

It is also said: the verse is among those with which the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) was addressed on the Night of the Ascension (ليلة المعراج), to ask the souls of the prophets (عليه السلام) — having met with them — whether they brought a religion other than the religion of monotheism.

More than one narration has been reported regarding this from the Imams of the People of the House (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), and it will reach you in the forthcoming "Tradition-based Discourse," Allah willing.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلَها كَلِمَةً باقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ "He made it a lasting word among his posterity": "It is said: the lasting word in his posterity is the Imamate until the Day of Judgment: narrated from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام)."

I say: regarding this meaning, there are other narrations, and the verse has been applied in some of them to the Imamate in the posterity of al-Husayn (عليه السلام).

Contemplation of the narrations shows that their basis is on returning the pronoun in "He made it" (جَعَلَها) to the guidance (الهداية) understood from His saying: "He will surely guide me" (سَيَهْدِينِ). It has already been mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي جاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِماماً "‘I am making you the Imam of mankind.’" (2:124) that the Imam's function is the guidance of people in the celestial realm (ملكوت) of their actions, in the sense of driving them toward Allah, the Glorified, by guiding them and bringing them to the degrees of proximity to Allah, the Glorified, and placing every possessor of an action in the station that his action requires. The reality of guidance is from Allah, the Glorified, and is attributed to him [the Imam] by following or by accident.

The actuality of the guidance descending from Allah to the people encompasses him first, then overflows from him to others. Thus, he has the most perfect guidance, and for others is that which is below it. What Ibrahim (عليه السلام) mentioned in his saying: "He will surely guide me" is an absolute guidance that accepts application to the most perfect levels of guidance, which is the Imam's share of it. Thus, it is the Imamate, and making it a lasting word in his posterity is making the Imamate so.

In al-Ihtijaj, from al-'Askari from his father (عليه السلام), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) was sitting one day in the courtyard of the Kaaba, when 'Abdullah ibn Umayyah al-Makhzumi said to him: 'If Allah had wanted to send a messenger to us, He would have sent one who is greater than us in wealth and better in state. Why was this Quran, which you claim Allah sent down to you and sent you with as a messenger, not sent down to a great man from the two cities: either al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah in Mecca, or 'Urwah ibn Mas'ud al-Thaqafi in Ta'if?'"

Then he (عليه السلام) mentioned in a long discourse the Messenger of Allah's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) answer to his saying, with what is in the meaning of the verses.

Then he said: "And that is His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ عَلى رَجُلٍ مِنَ الْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ 'And they said, ‘Why was this Quran not sent down to some great man from the two cities?’' Allah said: أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ 'Is it they who dispense the mercy of your Lord?' O Muhammad, نَحْنُ قَسَمْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا 'It is We who have dispensed among them their livelihood in the present life.' So We made some of us in need of others; We made this one in need of that one's wealth, and that one in need of this one's goods and his service.

So you see the greatest of kings and the richest of the wealthy in need of the poorest of the poor in some way — either a good with him that he does not have, or a service he is suited for that the king cannot manage to do without him, or a door of sciences and wisdom that he is poor toward benefiting from this poor man, who in turn needs the wealth of that wealthy king; while that king needs the knowledge of this poor man, or his opinion, or his expertise.

Then the king does not have the right to say: 'Why was the knowledge of this poor man not gathered with my wealth?' nor the poor man to say: 'Why was the wealth of this wealthy king not gathered with my opinion, expertise, knowledge, and the various arts of wisdom I handle?' Then the Exalted said: وَرَفَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجاتٍ لِيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضاً سُخْرِيًّا 'and raised some of them above others in rank, so that some may take others into service.'"

Then He said: "O Muhammad, وَرَحْمَتُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ مِمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ 'and the mercy of your Lord is better than what they amass,' meaning what these people amass of the wealth of the world."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib, he said: "I asked 'Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً 'Were it not that mankind would have become a single community.' He said: 'He meant by that the community of Muhammad — that they would all be upon one religion as disbelievers, لَجَعَلْنا لِمَنْ يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمنِ "We would have provided for those who disbelieve in the All-beneficent..."' until the end of the verse."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, with its chain of transmission from Yahya ibn Sa'id from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام), he said: "فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ 'Whether We take you away,' O Muhammad, from Mecca to Medina — then We shall either return you to it, and We are taking vengeance on them through 'Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: "'Abd al-Razzaq, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Qatadah regarding His saying: فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ فَإِنَّا مِنْهُمْ مُنْتَقِمُونَ 'Whether We take you away, We shall indeed take vengeance on them.' He said: 'Anas said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) passed away and the vengeance remained. Allah did not show His Prophet anything he disliked in his community until he was taken. No prophet was ever sent except that he saw the punishment in his community, except your Prophet; he saw what would afflict his community after him, so he was not seen laughing or expansive until he was taken.'"

I say: this meaning has been narrated from him, from 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, and from others through other paths.

In it [the same source]: "Ibn Mardawayh narrated through the path of Muhammad ibn Marwan from al-Kalbi from Abu Salih from Jabir ibn 'Abdillah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ فَإِنَّا مِنْهُمْ مُنْتَقِمُونَ 'Whether We take you away, We shall indeed take vengeance on them.' He said: 'It was revealed concerning 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, that he shall take vengeance on the oath-breakers (الناكثين) and the deviators (القاسطين) after me.'"

I say: the apparent meaning of the narration, the one before it, and what is in their meaning, is that the threat in the two verses is for those who deviate from the truth among the people of the Qiblah, rather than the disbelievers of Quraysh.

In al-Ihtijaj, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) in a long hadith in which he says: "As for His saying, the Exalted: وَسْئَلْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رُسُلِنا 'Ask those of Our messengers whom We sent before you,' this is among the proofs of our Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) that Allah gave him, and by which He made the proof mandatory upon the rest of His creation. For when He sealed the prophets with him and made him a messenger to all nations and all creeds, He specified him with the ascent to heaven during the Ascension (المعراج), and gathered the prophets for him on that day. So he learned from them what they were sent with, and they carried to him the determinations (عزائم) of Allah, His signs, and His proofs." The hadith.

I say: al-Qummi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir, with his chain of transmission from Abu al-Rabi' from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) in answer to what Nafi' ibn al-Azraq asked him. It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through paths from Sa'id ibn Jubayr, Ibn Jurayh, and Ibn Zayd.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَقَالَ إِنِّى رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٦)
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his elite. He said, ‘I am indeed an apostle of the Lord of all the worlds.’ (46)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَضْحَكُونَ (٤٧)
But when he brought them Our signs, they indeed laughed at them. (47)
وَمَا نُرِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ إِلَّا هِىَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا ۖ وَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْعَذَابِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٤٨)
We did not show them a sign but it was greater than the other, and We visited on them punishment so that they might come back. (48)
وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَ ٱلسَّاحِرُ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ (٤٩)
They would say, ‘O magician! Invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you [to remove this scourge]. We will indeed be guided [when it is removed].’ (49)
فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ (٥٠)
But when We lifted the punishment from them, behold, they would break their pledge. (50)
وَنَادَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ فِى قَوْمِهِۦ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِى مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهَـٰذِهِ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِىٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٥١)
And Pharaoh proclaimed to his people, saying, ‘O my people! Do not the kingdom of Egypt and these rivers that run at my feet belong to me? Do you not perceive? (51)
أَمْ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ (٥٢)
Am I not better than this wretch who cannot even speak clearly? (52)
فَلَوْلَآ أُلْقِىَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِّن ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جَآءَ مَعَهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ (٥٣)
Why no bracelets of gold have been thrown to him, nor any angels accompany him as escorts?’ (53)
فَٱسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُۥ فَأَطَاعُوهُ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٥٤)
Thus did he mislead his people and they obeyed him. Indeed, they were a transgressing lot. (54)
فَلَمَّآ ءَاسَفُونَا ٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٥٥)
So when they roused Our wrath, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all. (55)
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ سَلَفًا وَمَثَلًا لِّلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٥٦)
Thus We made them the vanguard and an example for posterity. (56)

(Commentary)

After mentioning their rebellion (الطغيان) — their exceeding of limits in disbelief and disobedience — following His granting them enjoyment of His blessings, and their dismissal of the truth brought by a manifest messenger as sorcery, and their saying: "Why was this Quran not sent down to some great man from the two cities?" (43:31), thereby preferring a man over the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) based on the abundance of his wealth, He sets forth for them a parable through the story of Moses (عليه السلام), Pharaoh, and his people. This occurred when Allah sent him to them with His dazzling signs (الآيات), yet they laughed at them and mocked them. Pharaoh, in his address to his people, argued that he was better than Moses because of his sovereignty over Egypt and the rivers flowing beneath him. Thus, he incited them to levity (استخفهم) — he manipulated their intellects to make them view the truth as light and insignificant — and they obeyed him. The end of their arrogance (الاستكبار) — the haughty refusal to submit to the truth — was that Allah took vengeance upon them and drowned them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَى بِآيَاتِنَا إِلَى فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَائِهِ فَقَالَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ — "Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his elite. He said, ‘I am indeed the apostle of the Lord of all the worlds.’" (43:46). The letter lam in لَقَدْ is for an oath (القسم), and the ba in His saying بِآيَاتِنَا denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة), and the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ بِآيَاتِنَا إِذَا هُمْ مِنْهَا يَضْحَكُونَ — "But when he brought them Our signs, behold, they laughed at them." (43:47). What is meant by his bringing the signs is the manifestation of miracles (المعجزات) to demonstrate his messengership (الرسالة). The laughter intended here is the laughter of mockery (الاستهزاء) out of disdain for the signs.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا نُرِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ إِلَّا هِيَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا — "We did not show them any sign but it was greater than its predecessor..." (43:48). The "sister" (الأخت) means the peer or like. His saying هِيَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا is an allusion (كناية) to the fact that every single one of them reached the ultimate degree in demonstrating the truthfulness of the messengership. The clause وَمَا نُرِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the pronoun in مِنْهَا. The meaning is: when he brought them the miracles, they laughed at them, while the situation was that each of them was perfect and complete in its miraculous nature and indication, without any deficiency or shortcoming.

And His saying: وَأَخَذْنَاهُمْ بِالْعَذَابِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ — "...and We seized them with punishment so that they might come back." (43:48). That is, in the hope that they would turn back from their arrogance to accepting his message. The punishment (العذاب) with which they were seized refers to the signs of the plague (الرجز) sent upon them, such as the years of famine, the shortage of fruits, the flood, the locusts, the lice, the frogs, and the blood—distinct signs (آيات مفصلات) as mentioned in Surah al-A'raf.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَا السَّاحِرُ ادْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِنْدَكَ إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ — "They said, ‘O magician! Invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you. We will indeed be guided.’" (43:49). The word ma in بِمَا عَهِدَ عِنْدَكَ is infinitive (مصدرية), meaning "by His covenant with you." This refers to His covenant to remove the punishment from them if they believed, as has been said, or to answer his prayer when he supplicates, as some have suggested.

Their address يَا أَيُّهَا السَّاحِرُ (O magician) was a speech of mockery arising from their arrogance. Similarly, they said, "Invoke your Lord," and did not say "Invoke our Lord" or "Invoke Allah" out of pride. The meaning is that they requested him to supplicate for the removal of the punishment from them and promised him that they would be guided.

It has been said that the meaning of "magician" (الساحر) in their convention was "scholar" (العالم), and that the magician was great in their eyes and they honored him, so it was not a term of dispraise. However, this is not the case; rather, they were mocking in their arrogance, as evidenced by their saying: "Invoke your Lord for us."

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذَابَ إِذَا هُمْ يَنْكُثُونَ — "But when We removed the punishment from them, behold, they broke their promise." (43:50). Breaking (النكث) is the violation of a covenant (العهد) and the breaking of a promise. Their promise was their saying: "We will indeed be guided."

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَادَى فِرْعَوْنُ فِي قَوْمِهِ قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهَذِهِ الْأَنْهَارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ — "Pharaoh proclaimed among his people, saying, ‘O my people! Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me and these rivers that flow at my feet? Do you not perceive?" (43:51). That is, he called out to them while he was among them. The word قَالَ (he said) is separated (فصل) because it stands as an answer to a question, as if it were asked: "What did he say?" and it was replied: "He said such-and-such."

His saying: وَهَذِهِ الْأَنْهَارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي (and these rivers that flow beneath me) means beneath my palace or from my garden in which my palace stands, elevated and high in construction. The clause وَهَذِهِ الْأَنْهَارُ is either a circumstantial clause or is coordinated (معطوف) with مُلْكُ مِصْرَ (the kingdom of Egypt), and the phrase تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي is a circumstantial description of the rivers. The rivers refer to the branches of the Nile.

His saying: أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ (Do you not perceive?) carries the meaning of repeating the previous interrogative in his saying: "Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me..."

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ هَذَا الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ — "Am I not better than this one who is a lowly wretch and can hardly speak plainly?" (43:52). The lowly (المهين) is the contemptible and weak, from mahanah (المهانة) meaning insignificance (الحقارة). He intends by "lowly" Moses (عليه السلام) due to his poverty and the raggedness of his state.

His saying: وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ (and can hardly speak plainly) means he does not express his intent clearly. Perhaps he described Moses (عليه السلام) this way based on how he was before his messengership, but Allah removed that from him due to His saying: "He said, ‘You have been granted your request, O Moses!’" (20:36) after his (عليه السلام) saying: "And untie the knot from my tongue, so that they might understand my speech." (20:27-28).

Regarding the word أَمْ (or/rather) at the beginning of the verse أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ, it is either disjunctive (منقطعة) to affirm his previous speech, meaning: "Rather, I am better than Moses because he is such-and-such," or it is conjunctive (متصلة), where one side of the hesitation is omitted along with the interrogative hamzah, the implied meaning being: "Is this one better or am I better...?" In al-Majma', it is said: "Sibawayh and al-Khalil said: 'I' (أنا) is coordinated by am to 'Do you not perceive?' because the meaning of 'I am better' is the meaning of 'or do you perceive?' It is as if he said: 'Do you not perceive or do you perceive?' because if they say to him: 'You are better than him,' they have become 'perceiving' in his view." End quote. That is, placing أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ in the position of "or do you perceive" is the placement of the effect (المسبب) in the position of the cause (السبب) or vice versa.

In any case, the reference to Moses with "this" (هذا) without mentioning him by name is for belittlement (التحقير), and describing him as الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ is for belittlement and to indicate his lack of superiority.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَوْ لَا أُلْقِيَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جَاءَ مَعَهُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ — "Why have not gold bracelets been cast upon him, or why have not the angels accompanied him in a procession?" (43:53). Bracelets (الأسورة) is the plural of siwar (سوار). Al-Raghib said: "It is the Arabized form of dastwarah." They said: "It was their custom that when they made a man a leader, they would deck him with a gold bracelet and place a gold collar around his neck." So the meaning is: "If he were a messenger and led the people by that, gold bracelets would have been cast upon him."

His saying: أَوْ جَاءَ مَعَهُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ (or why have not the angels accompanied him in a procession). It appears that accompaniment (الاقتران) means coming together (التقارن), like istibaq and istiwa' mean competing and being equal. The meaning is the angels coming with him in close proximity to testify to his messengership. This statement is among those repeated by the tongues of those who denied the messengers, such as their saying: "Why has not an angel been sent down to him to be a warner with him?" (25:7).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُ فَأَطاعُوهُ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ — "So he incited his people to levity and they obeyed him. Indeed they were a transgressing lot." (43:54). That is, he made light of the intellects and minds of his people. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا آسَفُونا انْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْناهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "So when they provoked Our wrath, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all." (43:55). Provoking wrath (الإيساف) means causing anger (الإغضاب). That is, when they angered Us by their transgression, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all. Anger (الغضب) from Allah is the will to punish (إرادة العقوبة).

His saying, the Exalted: فَجَعَلْناهُمْ سَلَفاً وَمَثَلاً لِلْآخِرِينَ — "And We made them a precedent and an example for the later [generations]." (43:56). The precedent (السلف) is the one who goes before. It appears that their being a precedent for the later ones refers to their preceding them in entering the Fire. The example (المثل) is the current saying that is used as a parable and a lesson. It appears that their being an example for them means they are something from which the later ones should take a lesson, if they were to reflect and take heed.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ, he said: "He did not make the speech plain."

In al-Tawhid, with its chain to Ahmad ibn Abi Abdillah, reaching to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَلَمَّا آسَفُونا انْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ, he said: إن الله لا يأسف كأسفنا ـ ولكنه خلق أولياء لنفسه يأسفون ويرضون ـ وهم مخلوقون مدبرون ـ فجعل رضاهم لنفسه رضى وسخطهم لنفسه سخطا ـ وذلك لأنه جعلهم الدعاة إليه والأدلاء عليه ـ فلذلك صاروا كذلك. وليس أن ذلك يصل إلى الله كما يصل إلى خلقه ـ ولكن هذا معنى ما قال من ذلك ، وقد قال أيضا من أهان لي وليا ـ فقد بارزني بالمحاربة ودعاني إليها ، وقال أيضا : مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ ، وقال أيضا : إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبايِعُونَكَ إِنَّما يُبايِعُونَ اللهَ وكل هذا وشبهه على ما ذكرت لك ، وهكذا الرضا والغضب وغيرهما من الأشياء مما يشاكل ذلك. ولو كان يصل إلى المكون الأسف والضجر ـ وهو الذي أحدثهما وأنشأهما ـ لجاز لقائل أن يقول : إن المكون يبيد يوما ـ لأنه إذا دخله الضجر والغضب دخله التغيير ـ فإذا دخله التغيير لم يؤمن عليه الإبادة ، ولو كان ذلك كذلك لم يعرف المكون من المكون ـ وإلا القادر من المقدور ولا الخالق من المخلوقين ـ تعالى الله عن هذا القول علوا كبيرا. هو الخالق للأشياء لا لحاجة ـ فإذا كان لا لحاجة استحال الحد والكيف فيه ـ فافهم ذلك إن شاء الله. — "Indeed, Allah does not grieve (يأسف) as we grieve. Rather, He created friends (أولياء) for Himself who grieve and are pleased—and they are created and governed beings. He made their pleasure His pleasure and their wrath His wrath. This is because He made them the callers to Him and the guides toward Him; therefore, they became as such. It is not that this [emotion] reaches Allah as it reaches His creation, but this is the meaning of what He said regarding that. He also said: 'Whoever insults a friend of Mine has challenged Me to combat and invited Me to it.' And He said: 'Whoever obeys the Messenger has certainly obeyed Allah.' And He said: 'Indeed, those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah.' All of this and its like are according to what I have mentioned to you. Such is the case with pleasure, wrath, and other similar things. If grief and annoyance reached the Originator (المكون)—and He is the one who brought them into being and originated them—it would be permissible for a speaker to say: 'The Originator shall one day perish,' because if annoyance and wrath entered Him, change (التغيير) would enter Him. And if change entered Him, He would not be secure from perishing. If that were so, the Originator would not be distinguished from the originated, nor the Powerful from the powerless, nor the Creator from the created. Exalted is Allah far above such a saying. He is the Creator of things not out of need; and since it is not out of need, limit (الحد) and modality (الكيف) are impossible for Him. So understand that, if Allah wills."

I say: A similar narration is reported in al-Kafi, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Isma'il ibn Bazi' from his uncle Hamzah ibn Bazi' from him (عليه السلام).

Ayahs

۞ وَلَمَّا ضُرِبَ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلًا إِذَا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ (٥٧)
When the Son of Mary was cited as an example, behold, your people raise an outcry. (57)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ ءَأَـٰلِهَتُنَا خَيْرٌ أَمْ هُوَ ۚ مَا ضَرَبُوهُ لَكَ إِلَّا جَدَلًۢا ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ خَصِمُونَ (٥٨)
They say, ‘Are our gods better or he?’ They cite him to you only for the sake of contention. Indeed, they are a contentious lot. (58)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ مَثَلًا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (٥٩)
He was just a servant whom We had blessed and made an exemplar for the Children of Israel. (59)
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَجَعَلْنَا مِنكُم مَّلَـٰٓئِكَةً فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَخْلُفُونَ (٦٠)
Had We wished We would have set angels in your stead to be [your] successors on the earth. (60)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَعِلْمٌ لِّلسَّاعَةِ فَلَا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِهَا وَٱتَّبِعُونِ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٦١)
[Say,] ‘Indeed he is a portent of the Hour; so do not doubt it and follow me. This is a straight path. (61)
وَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٦٢)
Do not let Satan bar you [from the way of Allah]. Indeed he is your manifest enemy.’ (62)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَ عِيسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (٦٣)
When Jesus brought those manifest proofs, he said, ‘I have certainly brought you wisdom, and [I have come] to make clear to you some of the things that you differ about. So be wary of Allah and obey me. (63)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٦٤)
Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path.’ (64)
فَٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَيْنِهِمْ ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ (٦٥)
But the factions differed among themselves. So woe to the wrongdoers for the punishment of a painful day. (65)

(Commentary)

This is an allusion to the story of Jesus (عيسى) after concluding the story of Moses (عليه‌السلام). Preceding it was the mention of their disputation with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding Jesus (عليه‌السلام), which was subsequently answered.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا ضُرِبَ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلاً إِذا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ — When the son of Mary was cited as an example, behold, your people raised an outcry because of it (43:57) — until His saying: خَصِمُونَ — a contentious lot (43:58). This verse, along with the following four or six verses, concerns the people's disputation regarding the example cited of the son of Mary. What is gained through contemplation (التدبر) upon these verses—considering that the Surah is Meccan and setting aside the narrations—is that the intent of His saying, "When the son of Mary was cited as an example," refers to what Allah revealed of his description at the beginning of Surah Maryam. Indeed, it is the only Meccan Surah in which the story of Jesus son of Mary (عليه‌السلام) is mentioned in detail. That Surah recounts the stories of several prophets in terms of Allah having bestowed His favor upon them, concluding their stories with His saying: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ — They are the ones whom Allah has blessed from among the prophets (19:58). Within these current verses occurs His saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنا عَلَيْهِ — He was only a servant on whom We had bestowed Our blessing (43:59), which serves as evidence that His saying, "When the son of Mary was cited as an example," is an allusion to what is contained in Surah Maryam.

The intent of His saying: إِذا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ — behold, your people raised an outcry because of it — with a kasrah on the ṣād (يَصِدُّونَ), meaning they clamored and laughed. This is a censure of the Quraysh for meeting a true example with mockery and derision. It has also been recited with a ḍammah on the ṣād (يَصُدُّونَ), meaning they turn away, which is more suitable for the subsequent sentence.

His saying: وَقالُوا أَآلِهَتُنا خَيْرٌ أَمْ هُوَ — And they said, ‘Are our gods better, or he?’ (43:58). The interrogation is for denial (الإنكار), meaning: "Are our gods better than the son of Mary?" It is as if when they heard his name and how the Quran describes him with blessing and honor, they turned away from what the Quran describes and took him according to the attribute he holds among the Christians—that he is a god and the son of a god. Thus, they retorted to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by saying that their gods were better than him. This is among the most absurd forms of disputation (الجدال), as if they were suggesting that the description of him in the Quran is of no account, and what the Christians possess is of no benefit, for their gods are better than him.

His saying: ما ضَرَبُوهُ لَكَ إِلَّا جَدَلاً — They did not cite him to you except for the sake of contention (43:58). That is, they did not direct this speech—"Are our gods better, or he?"—to you except for the sake of contention (الجدل), intending thereby to invalidate the aforementioned example even though it is true. بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ خَصِمُونَ — Indeed, they are a contentious lot (43:58); meaning they are firm in their hostility and persistent in it.

His saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنا عَلَيْهِ — He was only a servant on whom We had bestowed Our blessing (43:59). This is a refutation of what is implied by their saying, "Are our gods better, or he?"—namely, that he is the god of the Christians, as will follow.

Al-Zamakhshari stated in al-Kashshaf, as did many exegetes, and it is attributed to Ibn Abbas and others in the interpretation of the verse: When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ — Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell (21:98) to the Quraysh, they were deeply resentful. Ibn al-Ziba‘ra said: "O Muhammad, is this specifically for us and our gods, or for all nations?" He (عليه‌السلام) replied: "It is for you, your gods, and all nations."

He said: "I have defeated you, by the Lord of the Kaaba! Do you not claim that Jesus son of Mary is a prophet and praise him and his mother? You know that the Christians worship them both, and Uzayr is worshipped, and the angels are worshipped. If these are in the Fire, then we are content that we and our gods be with them." They rejoiced and laughed, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained silent. Then Allah revealed: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ — Indeed those to whom the [promise of] the best reward has gone forth from Us—they shall be kept away from it (21:101), and this verse was revealed.

The meaning would then be: When Ibn al-Ziba‘ra cited Jesus son of Mary as an example and disputed with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the Christians' worship of him, behold, your people—meaning the Quraysh—raised a clamor of joy and laughter at this example because of what they heard from him, thinking he had silenced the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They said: "Are our gods better, or he?" meaning: "Jesus is better in your view than our gods, so if he is the fuel of Hell, then the matter of our gods is trivial." They did not cite this example to you except for the sake of contention and to gain verbal victory, not to distinguish truth from falsehood.

The critique of this is that it was previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying: إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ — Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell (21:98), that this narration, with its various points of weakness and defect, is feeble and not to be relied upon. It was even reported from al-Hafiz Ibn Hajar that the hadith has no basis and is not found in any of the books of hadith, neither with a chain of transmission (مسند) nor without one. Although this story of Ibn al-Ziba‘ra is narrated through Shi'a channels in a manner free from such criticism, it does not mention the revelation of His saying: "When the son of Mary was cited as an example" in that context.

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of His saying: "the son of Mary was cited as an example" and His saying: "Are our gods better, or he?" does not harmonize well with that interpretation.

It has also been said: When they heard the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was (3:59), they said: "We are better guided than the Christians, for they worship a human being while we worship the angels"—referring to the lords of the idols—"so our gods are better than their god." Thus, the one who cited the example of the son of Mary was Allah, the Glorified, and their saying: "Are our gods better, or he?" was to favor their gods over Jesus, not the reverse as in the previous view.

The critique here is that the saying of the Exalted: "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam" is Medinan, while these verses—meaning His saying: "When the son of Mary was cited as an example" etc.—are Meccan verses from a Meccan Surah. Moreover, the basis of their statement—according to this view—is their favoring themselves over the Christians; thus, His saying: "He was only a servant on whom We had bestowed Our blessing" etc., would have no connection to what preceded it.

It has also been said: When they heard His saying: "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam," they clamored and said: "Muhammad only wants us to worship him as the Christians worship the Messiah, and our gods are better than him"—meaning Muhammad. This suffers from the same flaw as the previous view.

It has also been said: Their intent in saying: "Are our gods better, or he?" was to excuse themselves and escape the condemnation of their saying: "The angels are the daughters of Allah," and their worship of them. It is as if they said: "This was not an innovation on our part, for the Christians worship the Messiah and attribute him to Allah though he is a human, while we worship the angels and attribute them to Allah though they are superior to humans."

The critique here is that it fails to justify the phrasing of His saying: "When the son of Mary was cited as an example, behold, your people raised an outcry because of it." Furthermore, His saying: "He was only a servant on whom We had bestowed Our blessing" would, according to this view, have no connection to what preceded it, as in the previous two views.

It has also been said: The meaning of their saying: "Are our gods better, or he?" is that our case in worshipping gods is like the case of the Christians in worshipping the Messiah, so which of the two is better? The worship of our gods or the worship of the Messiah? If he says: "The worship of the Messiah is better," he has admitted to the worship of other than Allah; if he says: "The worship of the gods," it is the same; and if he says: "There is no good in the worship of the Messiah," he has diminished his status. The answer to this is that the Messiah's being singled out for a type of honor and blessing from Allah, the Exalted, does not necessitate the permissibility of worshipping him.

The critique here is that while this is not problematic in itself, the issue lies in whether the saying of the Exalted: "Are our gods better, or he?" actually points to this level of detail.

In al-Majma‘, among the views cited regarding the meaning of the verse: The fourth is what was narrated by the masters of the Ahl al-Bayt from Ali (عليه‌السلام) that he said: "I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) one day and found him among a group of the Quraysh. He looked at me and then said: 'O Ali, your likeness in this nation is like that of Jesus son of Mary; a people loved him and went to extremes in their love for him, so they perished; and a people hated him and went to extremes in their hatred for him, so they perished; and a people were moderate regarding him, so they were saved.'" This was hard for them to bear, so they laughed and said: "He likens him to the prophets and messengers!" Then the verse was revealed.

I say: The narration does not address the justification for their saying: "Are our gods better, or he?" If the story were the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول), the meaning of the sentence would be: "If we follow our gods and obey our leaders, it is better than following Ali so that he may rule over us, or better than following Muhammad so that his cousin may rule over us."

It is possible that His saying: "And they said, ‘Are our gods better, or he?’" etc., is a new sentence (استئناف), and what was revealed regarding the story is His saying: "When the son of Mary was cited as an example..." the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنا عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلْناهُ مَثَلاً لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ — He was only a servant on whom We had bestowed Our blessing, and We made him an example for the Children of Israel (43:59). What the context requires is that the pronoun refers to the son of Mary. The meaning of his being an "example"—according to what has been said—is his being a wondrous divine sign (آية) whose mention spreads like well-known proverbs (الأمثال السائرة).

The meaning is: The son of Mary was nothing but a servant manifesting servitude (العبودية), upon whom We bestowed favor through prophethood, supporting him with the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), performing manifest miracles at his hands, and other such things. We made him a wondrous, extraordinary sign by which We describe the truth to the Children of Israel.

This meaning, as you can see, is a refutation of their saying: "Are our gods better, or he?" which apparently favors their gods in their divinity over the Messiah (عليه‌السلام) in his divinity. Its summary is that the Messiah was not a god such that his status in divinity should be considered; rather, he was a servant upon whom Allah bestowed what He bestowed. As for their gods, the Quran's view of them is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَجَعَلْنا مِنْكُمْ مَلائِكَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ يَخْلُفُونَ — Had We wished, We would have set in your stead angels to be [Our] successors on the earth (43:60). It appears that the verse is connected to what preceded it, narrated to remove the perceived impossibility that a human could be clothed in the perfection that the Quran recounts regarding Jesus (عليه‌السلام)—such as creating birds, reviving the dead, and speaking to people in the cradle, among other things—thereby being like the angels who mediate in giving life, causing death, providing sustenance, and other types of governance (التدبير), while remaining a servant who is not worshipped and a created being (مألوه) who is not a god. For this type of perfection, according to the pagans, is exclusive to the angels and is the basis of their divinity and their being worshipped. In short, they deem it impossible for a human to be clothed in this type of perfection which they reserve for angels.

Thus, the answer was given that it is for Allah to purify a human and cleanse him from the stains of disobedience such that his inner reality becomes the inner reality of the angels; thus his outer form is that of a human while his inner reality is that of an angel, living on the earth, succeeding his like and being succeeded by his like, and manifesting what is manifested by the angels.

Accordingly, the "from" (مِنْ) in His saying: مِنْكُمْ — from you — is for partitivity (التبعيض), and His saying: يَخْلُفُونَ — succeeding — means they succeed one another.

In al-Majma‘, it is stated that the "from" (مِنْ) in His saying: "from you" conveys the meaning of substitution (البدلية), as in the poetic verse:

Would that we had, instead of the water of Zamzam, a drink Cooled, having spent the night upon the mountain peak

And His saying: "succeeding" means they succeed the children of Adam and become successors to them. The meaning would be: "Had We wished, We would have destroyed you and placed in your stead angels to inhabit the earth, cultivate it, and worship Allah."

The critique of this is that it does not harmonize with the structure of the verse as well as the former view.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَعِلْمٌ لِلسَّاعَةِ فَلا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِها وَاتَّبِعُونِ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ — Indeed he is a means of knowing the Hour: so do not doubt it, and follow me. This is a straight path (43:61). The pronoun in إِنَّهُ — Indeed he/it — refers to Jesus (عليه‌السلام). The intent of "knowledge" (العلم) is that by which something is known. The meaning is: Indeed, through Jesus, the Hour is known, by his creation without a father and his reviving the dead; thus it is known through him that the Hour is possible. Therefore, do not doubt the Hour and do not harbor any uncertainty regarding it at all.

It has been said: The meaning of his being a "sign for the Hour" is his being among its portents (أشراطها), descending to the earth, whereby the proximity of the Hour is known.

It has also been said: The pronoun refers to the Quran, and its being a "sign for the Hour" is its being the last of the books revealed from heaven.

In both of these latter views, the clarity of the derivation (التفريع) in His saying: فَلا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِها — so do not doubt it — is obscured.

His saying: وَاتَّبِعُونِ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ — and follow me. This is a straight path (43:61). It has been said: This is from the speech of the Exalted, and the meaning is: "Follow My guidance" or "My law" or "My Messenger." It has also been said: It is from the speech of the Messenger by His command, the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَصُدَّنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ — And do not let Satan bar you. Indeed he is your manifest enemy (43:62). الصد — Barring — is turning away (الصرف), and the rest is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جاءَ عِيسى بِالْبَيِّناتِ قالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِالْحِكْمَةِ — When Jesus brought the manifest proofs, he said, ‘I have certainly brought you wisdom (43:63) etc. The intent of "manifest proofs" (البينات) is the clear signs from the miracles, and by "wisdom" (الحكمة) is meant the divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) consisting of true beliefs and virtuous ethics.

His saying: وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ — and to clarify for you some of what you differ about (43:63); meaning in its ruling regarding events and actions. Although that which they differ about is more general than beliefs—which are differed upon as to whether they are true or false—and events and actions—which are differed upon as to the lawfulness of their ruling—it is more appropriate, given the preceding "I have brought you wisdom," that what they differed about be restricted to events and actions. And Allah knows best.

It has been said: The intent of His saying: "some of what you differ about" is all of what you differ about. This is as you can see.

It has also been said: The intent is: "to clarify for you the matters of your religion, excluding the matters of your worldly life." There is no evidence for this from the wording of the verse nor from the context.

His saying: فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ — So be wary of Allah and obey me (43:63). He attributed godwariness (التقوى) to Allah and obedience to himself to establish that he claims nothing but the messengership (الرسالة).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ — Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path (43:64). This is a call from him to worship Allah alone, and that He is his Lord and the Lord of them all, and a completion of the proof (إتمام للحجة) against those who claim his divinity.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاخْتَلَفَ الْأَحْزابُ مِنْ بَيْنِهِمْ فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْ عَذابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ — But the factions differed among themselves. So woe to the wrongdoers from the punishment of a painful day (43:65). The pronoun in مِنْ بَيْنِهِمْ — among themselves — refers to those to whom Jesus (عليه‌السلام) was sent. The meaning is: The factions branching out from among his nation differed regarding the matter of Jesus: from the disbeliever in him who spoke against him, to the believer in him who went to extremes (غال) regarding him, to the moderate one who adhered to the middle course.

His saying: فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْ عَذابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ — So woe to the wrongdoers from the punishment of a painful day — is a threat and a warning to both the detractor (القالي) among them and the extremist (الغالي).

Ayahs

هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٦٦)
Do they not consider that the Hour may overtake them suddenly while they are unaware? (66)
ٱلْأَخِلَّآءُ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٦٧)
On that day, friends will be one another’s enemies, except for the Godwary. (67)
يَـٰعِبَادِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَلَآ أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ (٦٨)
[They will be told,] ‘O My servants! Today you will have no fear, nor will you grieve (68)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ مُسْلِمِينَ (٦٩)
—those who believed in Our signs and had been Muslims. (69)
ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ أَنتُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُكُمْ تُحْبَرُونَ (٧٠)
Enter paradise, you and your spouses, rejoicing.’ (70)
يُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِصِحَافٍ مِّن ذَهَبٍ وَأَكْوَابٍ ۖ وَفِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِيهِ ٱلْأَنفُسُ وَتَلَذُّ ٱلْأَعْيُنُ ۖ وَأَنتُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٧١)
They will be served around with golden dishes and goblets, and therein will be whatever the souls desire and eyes delight in. ‘You will remain in it [forever]. (71)
وَتِلْكَ ٱلْجَنَّةُ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٧٢)
That is the paradise you have been given to inherit for what you used to do. (72)
لَكُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ كَثِيرَةٌ مِّنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٧٣)
There are abundant fruits for you in it, from which you will eat.’ (73)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ فِى عَذَابِ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدُونَ (٧٤)
Indeed the guilty will remain [forever] in the punishment of hell. (74)
لَا يُفَتَّرُ عَنْهُمْ وَهُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ (٧٥)
It will not be lightened for them, and they will be despondent in it. (75)
وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٧٦)
We did not wrong them, but they themselves were wrongdoers. (76)
وَنَادَوْا۟ يَـٰمَـٰلِكُ لِيَقْضِ عَلَيْنَا رَبُّكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنَّكُم مَّـٰكِثُونَ (٧٧)
They will call out, ‘O Malik! Let your Lord finish us off!’ He will say, ‘Indeed you will stay on.’ (77)
لَقَدْ جِئْنَـٰكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَـٰرِهُونَ (٧٨)
‘We certainly brought you the truth, but most of you were averse to the truth.’ (78)

(Commentary)

This is a return to the warning (الإنذار) directed at the people, containing an intimidation regarding the Hour (الساعة) and an indication of what the state of the God-conscious (المتقين) and the guilty (المجرمين) will lead to therein, in terms of reward and punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ "Do they await anything but the Hour, that it should overtake them suddenly while they are unaware?" (43:66). Looking (النظر) means waiting (الانتظار), and suddenness (البغتة) means unexpectedly (الفجأة). What is meant by their lack of awareness (عدم شعورهم) of it is their heedlessness (الغفلة) toward it due to their preoccupation with worldly affairs, as the Exalted said: ما يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ "They do not await but a single Cry that will seize them while they are disputing" (36:49). Thus, the meaning is not redundant in His saying: "suddenly while they are unaware."

The meaning is: These disbelievers (الكفار), by their disbelief and their denial of the signs of Allah, await nothing but for the Hour to come upon them unexpectedly while they are heedless of it, occupied with their worldly matters. That is, their condition is like that of one threatened by destruction who fails to seek any means of salvation and sits waiting for ruin. The speech contains a metonymy (كناية) for their lack of concern for faith in the truth (الحق), through which they could escape the painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: الْأَخِلَّاءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ "On that day, friends will be one another’s enemies, except for the God-conscious" (43:67). Friends (الأخلاء) is the plural of khalīl (خليل), which refers to a friend who fills the gaps (خلة) and needs of his companion. It appears that "friends" refers to the term in its absolute sense, encompassing both friendship and mutual love for the sake of Allah—as in the friendship of the God-conscious (المتقين), the people of the Hereafter—and friendship for other than Him, as in the friendship of the people of this world. Thus, the exception of the God-conscious is a connected exception (متصل).

The reason for the enmity between friends other than the God-conscious is that one of the requirements of friendship is for one friend to assist the other in his significant affairs. If this is for other than the sake of Allah, it involves assistance toward eternal wretchedness (الشقوة) and everlasting punishment, as the Exalted said, narrating the words of the wrongdoers (الظالمين) on the Day of Resurrection: يا وَيْلَتى لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلاناً خَلِيلاً لَقَدْ أَضَلَّنِي عَنِ الذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جاءَنِي "Woe to me! I wish I had not taken so-and-so as a friend! He certainly led me astray from the Reminder after it had come to me" (25:28-29). As for the friends among the God-conscious, their friendship is strengthened and benefits them on that day.

In the Prophetic report (الخبر النبوي): إذا كان يوم القيامة انقطعت الأرحام وقلت الأنساب ـ وذهبت الأخوة إلا الأخوة في الله ـ وذلك قوله : «الْأَخِلَّاءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ» — "When the Day of Resurrection comes, kinship ties will be severed, lineages will diminish, and brotherhoods will vanish except for brotherhood for the sake of Allah; and that is His saying: 'On that day, friends will be one another’s enemies, except for the God-conscious.'" (1)

His saying, the Exalted: يا عِبادِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَلا أَنْتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ "O My servants! Today you shall have no fear, nor shall you grieve" (43:68). This is from His address to them on the Day of Resurrection, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: "Enter paradise," etc. In this address is an assurance for them against every disliked thing, whether potential or certain; for the object of fear (الخوف) is a potential disliked thing, and the object of grief (الحزن) is a certain disliked thing. When both are removed, all distress is removed.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِآياتِنا وَكانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ "[Those] who believed in Our signs and had submitted" (43:69). The relative pronoun (الموصول) is an appositive (بدل) for the addressed noun in "O My servants" or an attribute (صفة) for it. The signs (الآيات) are everything that points toward Him, the Exalted, including a prophet, a book, or any other indicative sign. What is meant by submission (الإسلام) is surrendering (التسليم) to the will and command of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ أَنْتُمْ وَأَزْواجُكُمْ تُحْبَرُونَ "Enter paradise, you and your spouses, rejoicing" (43:70). The apparent meaning of the command to enter Paradise is that "spouses" (الأزواج) refers to the believing women from the world, rather than the ḥūr al-ʿīn (الحور العين), because the latter are already in Paradise and do not leave it.

Rejoicing (الحبور) — as has been said — is the joy (السرور) whose effect and trace (حباره) appear on the face; and al-ḥabrah (الحبرة) is adornment and beauty of form. The meaning is: Enter Paradise, you and your believing spouses, while you are experiencing a joy whose trace appears on your faces, or while you are adorned with the finest ornaments.

His saying, the Exalted: يُطافُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِصِحافٍ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ وَأَكْوابٍ "They will be served with golden trays and goblets..." (43:71). Trays (الصحاف) is the plural of ṣaḥfah (صحفة), which is a large bowl (القصعة) or something slightly smaller. Goblets (الأكواب) is the plural of kūb (كوب), which is a jug without a handle. The mention of trays and goblets is an indication of their enjoyment of food and drink.

The shift (الالتفات) to the third person (الغيبة) in His saying: "They will be served" (يُطافُ عَلَيْهِمْ) between the two direct addresses "Enter paradise" and "you will abide therein forever" is to magnify (تفخيم) their honor and the favors bestowed upon them—implying that this state is such that it ought to be mentioned to others so that their longing (اغتباطهم) may increase and the truth of what they were promised may become manifest.

And His saying: وَفِيها ما تَشْتَهِيهِ الْأَنْفُسُ وَتَلَذُّ الْأَعْيُنُ "...and in it there is whatever the souls desire and the eyes delight in" (43:71). It appears that what the souls desire (ما تشتهيه الأنفس) refers to that which natural appetite (الشهوة الطبيعية) attaches to, such as things tasted, smelled, heard, or touched—things in which humans and animals generally share. What the eyes delight in (ما تلذه الأعين) refers to beauty and adornment, the delight of which is almost exclusive to humans, such as pleasant vistas, beautiful faces, and magnificent clothing. For this reason, the expression was varied: He expressed what relates to the souls as "desire" (الاشتهاء) and what relates to the eyes as "delight" (اللذة). Within these two categories, all psychological pleasures (اللذائذ النفسانية) known to us are encompassed.

It is possible that spiritual and intellectual pleasures (اللذائذ الروحية العقلية) are included in "what the eyes delight in," for spiritual delight is considered a form of the "vision of the heart."

It is said in al-Majmaʿ: "Allah, the Glorified, has combined in His saying: 'whatever the souls desire and the eyes delight in' that which, if all creatures were to gather to describe the types of bliss in Paradise, they could not add anything beyond what these two descriptions encompass." End quote.

And His saying: وَأَنْتُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ "...and you will abide in it forever" (43:71) is an announcement, a promise, and glad tidings of immortality (الخلود). In the knowledge of this, they have a spiritual pleasure that cannot be compared to anything else nor measured by any scale.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتِلْكَ الْجَنَّةُ الَّتِي أُورِثْتُمُوها بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ "That is the paradise you have been given to inherit for what you used to do" (43:72). It is said the meaning is: You were given it because of your deeds. It is also said: You inherited it from the disbelievers, who would have entered it had they believed and performed righteous deeds. The discussion of these two meanings has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْوارِثُونَ "It is they who will be the inheritors" (23:10).

His saying, the Exalted: لَكُمْ فِيها فاكِهَةٌ كَثِيرَةٌ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ "There is abundant fruit for you in it, of which you eat" (43:73). He added fruit to the previously mentioned food and drink to complete the enumeration of blessings. The word "of" (من) in "of which you eat" (مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض), and it is not without an indication that it is not exhausted by eating.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْمُجْرِمِينَ فِي عَذابِ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدُونَ لا يُفَتَّرُ عَنْهُمْ وَهُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ "Indeed the guilty will abide in the punishment of hell forever. It will not be lightened for them, and they will be despairing therein" (43:74-75). What is meant by the guilty (المجرمين) are those characterized by guilt (الإجرام), which is broader than disbelievers (الكفار). This is supported by its being mentioned in contrast to the God-conscious (المتقين), which is more specific than the believers (المؤمنين).

Lightening (التفتير) means reduction or mitigation. Despair (الإبلاس) is hopelessness (اليأس), and their despair is either of mercy or of exiting the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما ظَلَمْناهُمْ وَلكِنْ كانُوا هُمُ الظَّالِمِينَ "We did not wrong them, but they were the wrongdoers" (43:76). This is because He, the Exalted, recompensed them for their deeds, but they wronged themselves by leading their souls, through their actions, to the place of wretchedness and destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنادَوْا يا مالِكُ لِيَقْضِ عَلَيْنا رَبُّكَ قالَ إِنَّكُمْ ماكِثُونَ "They will call out, ‘O Mālik! Let your Lord finish us off.’ He will say, ‘You are here to stay’" (43:77). Mālik (مالك) is the angel who is the keeper of the Fire, according to reports narrated through both Sunni and Shi'a chains.

Their addressing Mālik regarding what they ask of Allah, the Glorified, is because they are veiled from Him, as the Exalted said: كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَنْ رَبِّهِمْ يَوْยَمَئِذٍ لَمَحْجُوبُونَ "No indeed! They will be veiled from their Lord on that day" (83:15), and He said: قالَ اخْسَؤُا فِيها وَلا تُكَلِّمُونِ "He will say, ‘Begone into it, and do not speak to Me!’" (23:108).

Thus, the meaning is that they ask Mālik to ask Allah to "finish them off." What is meant by "finishing them off" (القضاء عليهم) is putting them to death; they desire death in the sense of non-existence (الانعدام) and annihilation (البطلان) so that they may be saved from the wretchedness and painful punishment they are in. This is a manifestation of their worldly dispositions (ملكاتهم الدنيوية), for in the world they viewed death as non-existence and loss, not as a transition from one abode to another. Thus, they ask for death in the sense that was ingrained in their souls; otherwise, they have already died and witnessed what its reality is.

His saying: قالَ إِنَّكُمْ ماكِثُونَ "He will say, ‘You are here to stay’" (43:77). That is, in the wretched life and painful punishment you are in. The speaker is Mālik, in response to their request.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ جِئْناكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كارِهُونَ "We certainly brought you the truth, but most of you were averse to the truth" (43:78). The apparent meaning is that this is a continuation of Mālik's speech, speaking on behalf of the angels, as he is one of them. It is also said to be the speech of the Exalted, but this is rendered unlikely by the fact that they are veiled from their Lord on that day and Allah does not speak to them.

The address is to the people of the Fire insofar as they are human beings. Thus, the meaning is: We certainly brought you, O assembly of humans, the truth (الحق), but most of you—namely the guilty—are averse to the truth.

It is said that "the truth" (الحق) refers to truth in an absolute sense—any truth whatsoever—which they dislike and flee from. As for the specific truth (الحق المعهود), which is monotheism (التوحيد) or the Quran, all of them are averse to it and repulsed by it.

What is meant by their aversion to the truth is aversion according to the secondary nature (الطبع الثاني) acquired through acts of disobedience and sins, not according to the primary nature (الطبع الأول), which is the primordial nature (الفطرة) upon which people were created. For if they were averse to it according to that nature, they would not have been tasked with accepting it. The Exalted said: لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ "There is no altering Allah’s creation" (30:30), and He said: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها "By the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its Godwariness" (91:7-8).

It appears from the verse that the criterion for felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاء) is the acceptance or rejection of the truth.

(1) Narrated in al-Durr al-Manthūr regarding the verse, on the authority of Saʿd ibn Muʿādh.

Ayahs

أَمْ أَبْرَمُوٓا۟ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّا مُبْرِمُونَ (٧٩)
Have they settled on some [devious] plan? Indeed, We too are settling [on Our plans]. (79)
أَمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّا لَا نَسْمَعُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْوَىٰهُم ۚ بَلَىٰ وَرُسُلُنَا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ (٨٠)
Do they suppose that We do not hear their secret thoughts and their secret talks? Yes indeed [We do]! And with them are Our messengers, writing down [everything]. (80)
قُلْ إِن كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَلَدٌ فَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْعَـٰبِدِينَ (٨١)
Say, ‘If the All-beneficent had offspring, I would have been the first to worship [him].’ (81)
سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (٨٢)
Clear is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, the Lord of the Throne, of whatever they allege [concerning Him]! (82)
فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا۟ وَيَلْعَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ (٨٣)
So leave them to gossip and play until they encounter the day they are promised. (83)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ إِلَـٰهٌ وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَـٰهٌ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٨٤)
It is He who is God in the sky, and God on the earth, and He is the All-Wise, the All-Knowing. (84)
وَتَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَعِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٨٥)
Blessed is He to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and with Him is the knowledge of the Hour, and to Him you will be brought back. (85)
وَلَا يَمْلِكُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٨٦)
Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who are witness to the truth and who know [for whom to intercede]. (86)
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٨٧)
If you ask them, ‘Who created them?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Then where do they stray? (87)
وَقِيلِهِۦ يَـٰرَبِّ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمٌ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٨)
And his plaint: ‘My Lord! Indeed these are a people who will not have faith!’ (88)
فَٱصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلَـٰمٌ ۚ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٨٩)
So disregard them, and say, ‘Peace!’ Soon they will know. (89)

(Commentary)

This is a return to the previous discourse, containing a rebuke for their intended plots against the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and a threat that Allah will outplot them. It also negates the "son" they attribute to Him, invalidates the claim of any partner whatsoever, and establishes absolute Lordship (الربوبية) — the exclusive right of Allah to sustain, govern, and direct all creation — for Allah alone. The Surah concludes with threats and warnings of punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ أَبْرَمُوا أَمْراً فَإِنَّا مُبْرِمُونَ "Or have they settled on some course? Then We [too] will settle [on Our course]." (43:79). Ratification (الإبرام) is the opposite of revocation (النقض); it means to make something firm or decisive. The word "Or" (أَمْ) here is disjunctive (منقطعة).

The meaning, based on the context of this verse and the one following it, is: Rather, they have firmly devised a plot against you, O Muhammad, so We are firmly executing a counter-plot against them. Thus, the verse carries the same meaning as His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يُرِيدُونَ كَيْداً فَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هُمُ الْمَكِيدُونَ "Do they unroll a plot? But it is the faithless who are the victims of [their own] plot." (52:42).

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّا لا نَسْمَعُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْواهُمْ بَلى وَرُسُلُنا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ "Do they rest assured that We do not hear their secrets and their private confabulations? Yes [We do], and Our messengers are with them, writing down." (43:80). The secret (السر) is what they conceal within their hearts, and the private confabulation (النجوى) is what they whisper to one another such that no one else hears them. Since the secret is the "speech of the soul," the knowledge of both the secret and the private confabulation is expressed here as "hearing."

His saying: بَلى وَرُسُلُنا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ "Yes [We do], and Our messengers are with them, writing down" means: Yes, indeed, We hear their secrets and their private whispers, and Our messengers, who are entrusted with recording their deeds, are with them writing that down.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنْ كانَ لِلرَّحْمنِ وَلَدٌ فَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْعابِدِينَ "Say, ‘If the All-beneficent had a son, I would be the first of [his] worshippers.’" (43:81). This is an invalidation of the divinity of a son by invalidating the very basis of his existence, through the Prophet's knowledge that such a son does not exist. The use of the conditional "if" (إِنْ) rather than the hypothetical "if" (لو) — which denotes impossibility — was employed here (even though the context might have suggested the latter) to draw them down from the level of stubbornness to the stage of fair-mindedness.

The meaning is: Say to them, if the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) had a son as they claim, I would be the first to worship him in fulfillment of the right of his sonship and his essential similarity (مسانخته) to his father. However, I know that He has no son, and therefore I do not worship one—not out of hatred or the like.

Other meanings have been proposed for this verse:

One is that the meaning is: If Allah had a son as you claim, I would worship Allah alone and not worship the son you claim.

Another is that "if" (إِنْ) is negating (نافية), meaning: "Say: The All-Beneficent has no son, and I am the first of the worshippers who profess His oneness (الموحدين) among you."

Another is that "worshippers" (الْعابِدِينَ) is derived from abada (عَبَدَ) in the sense of "to disdain" (أَنَفَ). The meaning would then be: "Say: If the All-Beneficent had a son, I would be the first to disdain and recoil from his worship, because one who begets can only be a body, and corporeality (الجسمية) contradicts divinity (الألوهية)."

Another is that the meaning is: Just as I am not the first to worship Allah, likewise Allah has no son. That is, if it were permissible for you to claim that impossibility, it would be permissible for me to claim this impossibility. There are other opinions as well, but the apparent meaning of the verse is what we presented first.

His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحانَ رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ رَبِّ الْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ "Immaculate is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, the Lord of the Throne, from what they allege!" (43:82). This is a glorification (تسبيح) of Him, the Glorified, far above what they attribute to Him. It appears that "Lord of the Throne" (رَبِّ الْعَرْشِ) is an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for "Lord of the heavens and the earth," because what is meant by the heavens and the earth is the totality of the witnessed world, which is the Throne (العرش) of His kingdom over which He is settled (استوى), and within which He judges and directs its affairs.

This also contains an allusion to an argument for monotheism (الوحدانية). Since creation (الخلق) is exclusive to Him, the Exalted—even by the admission of the opponent—and creation is among the affairs of the Throne of His kingdom, and since direction (التدبير) — the management and regulation of the affairs of the universe — proceeds from creation and origination (الإيجاد) (as it is the origination of the system running between creatures), then direction is also among the affairs of His Throne. Thus, His Lordship over the Throne is Lordship over all the heavens and the earth.

His saying, the Exalted: فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَيَلْعَبُوا حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ "So leave them to gossip and play until they encounter their day which they are promised." (43:83). This is a general threat to them, by commanding the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to turn away from them until they encounter that which he warns them of: the punishment of the Day of Resurrection.

The meaning is: Leave them to indulge in their falsehoods and play in their worldly life, occupied with that until they meet their day which they are promised, which is the Day of Resurrection, as mentioned in the previous verses: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ "Do they await anything but the Hour..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي فِي السَّماءِ إِلهٌ وَفِي الْأَرْضِ إِلهٌ وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ "It is He who is God in the heaven and God on the earth, and He is the All-wise, the All-knowing." (43:84). That is, He is the one who is God (إله) in the heaven, deserving of worship, and He is God on the earth; meaning He alone is the one deserving of the worship of the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth. The repetition of "God" (إله), as has been said, provides emphasis and indicates that His being God in the heaven and the earth means that His divinity (ألوهيته) relates to them, not that He is settled within them or within one of them.

The verse stands in opposition to what the idolaters establish—a god or gods for each of the heavens and the earth. The conclusion of the verse with His saying: وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ "and He is the All-wise, the All-knowing," which denotes exclusivity (الحصر), is an allusion to His oneness in Lordship, which necessitates wisdom (الحكمة) and knowledge (العلم).

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَبارَكَ الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما وَعِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ "Blessed is He to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and with Him is the knowledge of the Hour, and to Him you will be brought back." (43:85). This is praise for Him, the Exalted, through "blessedness" (التبارك), which is His being the source of abundant good.

Each of the three mentioned attributes is an argument for His uniqueness in Lordship. As for His ownership (ملك) of everything, it is clear, for Lordship belongs to the one who directs the affair, and direction belongs to the owner. As for the exclusivity of the knowledge of the Hour (علم الساعة) to Him, it is because the Hour is the ultimate destination toward which everything travels; how could it be proper for one who has no knowledge of the end of their journey to be the Lord of things? Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Lord of things, not those whom they call upon. As for the return of people to Him, the return is for reckoning and recompense, which is the final stage of direction. Therefore, He to whom is the return, to Him belongs the direction, and He to whom belongs the direction, to Him belongs Lordship.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and who have knowledge." (43:86). The context is one of generality; thus, what is meant by "those whom they invoke"—meaning those they worship besides Him—is every deity other than Him, the Exalted, including angels, jinn, humans, and others.

What is meant by "the truth" (الْحَقِ) is the truth that is monotheism (التوحيد), and "bearing witness" to it is the confession of it. What is meant by His saying: وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ "and who have knowledge," where knowledge is mentioned absolutely, is their knowledge of the reality of the state of the one for whom they intercede and the reality of his deeds, as He said: لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَقالَ صَواباً "they shall not speak, save him whom the All-beneficent permits and who says what is right" (78:38). Since this is the state of the intercessors—that they do not possess intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — except after bearing witness to the truth, they shall not intercede except for the people of monotheism, as He said: وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى "and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased" (21:28).

The verse explicitly affirms the existence of intercession.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ فَأَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ "If you ask them, ‘Who created them?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Then where are they being led astray?" (43:87). That is, until when will they be turned away from the truth, which is monotheism, toward falsehood, which is polytheism (الشرك)? This is because they admit that there is no creator but Allah, and direction (التدبير), which is the basis of Lordship, is inseparable from creation, as has been clarified repeatedly. Thus, the Lord who is worshipped is the one in whose hand is creation, and He is Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقِيلِهِ يا رَبِّ إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ قَوْمٌ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ "And his plea: ‘O my Lord! Indeed these are a people who will not believe.’" (43:88). The pronoun in "his plea" (قِيلِهِ) refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله without doubt. Qīl (قِيل) is a verbal noun like qawl (قول) and qāl (قال). "His plea" (قِيلِهِ) is coordinated (معطوف) — according to what has been said — with "the Hour" in His saying: وَعِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ "and with Him is the knowledge of the Hour." The meaning would be: And with Him is the knowledge of his saying: "O my Lord! Indeed these are a people who will not believe."

His saying, the Exalted: فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلامٌ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ "So disregard them, and say, ‘Peace!’ Soon they will know." (43:89). This is a command to turn away from them and an expression of despair regarding their faith. His saying: قُلْ سَلامٌ "and say, ‘Peace!’" means: Bid them a farewell of leaving them alone, without having any further concern for them. In His saying: فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ "Soon they will know," there is a threat and a warning of punishment.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ihtijaj, narrated from Imam Ali عليه‌السلام in a long tradition in which he says: "His saying: إِنْ كانَ لِلرَّحْمنِ وَلَدٌ فَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْعابِدِينَ means 'the first of the deniers' (الجاحدين). The interpretation (التأويل) of this saying has an inner meaning (باطن) that is opposite to its outer meaning (ظاهر)."

I say: It appears that what is meant is that it is contrary to what the word "worshipper" (عابد) typically refers to when used absolutely.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Hisham ibn al-Hakam, who said: Abu Shakir al-Daysani said: "There is a verse in the Quran that supports our view." I asked: "And what is it?" He said: هُوَ الَّذِي فِي السَّماءِ إِلهٌ وَفِي الْأَرْضِ إِلهٌ "It is He who is God in the heaven and God on the earth." I did not know how to answer him, so I went for the Hajj and informed Abu Abdillah [Imam al-Sadiq] عليه‌السلام. He said: "This is the speech of a wicked heretic (زنديق). When you return to him, ask: 'What is your name in Kufa?' He will say: 'So-and-so.' Then ask: 'What is your name in Basra?' He will say: 'So-and-so.' Then say: 'Likewise is Allah our Lord; in the heaven He is God, on the earth He is God, in the seas He is God, in the deserts He is God, and in every place He is God.'"

He [Hisham] said: "So I returned and went to Abu Shakir and informed him. He said: 'This [answer] was carried from the Hijaz.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ, he said: "They are those who were worshipped in the world—they do not possess intercession for those who worshipped them."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Abu Hashim al-Ja'fari, who said: I asked Abu Ja'far the Second [Imam al-Jawad] عليه‌السلام: "What is the meaning of 'the One' (الواحد)?" He replied: "The consensus of tongues upon His oneness (الوحدانية), because of His saying: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ 'If you ask them, "Who created them?" they will surely say, "Allah."'"
